title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: mystery-caller study (also called audit study correspondence study) controlled experiment used detect discrimination health-care access. central idea elegantly simple: trained research callers—posing prospective patients—telephone physician offices attempt schedule new-patient appointment. callers follow standardised script, vary characteristic investigation (commonly insurance type), record outcome call, design provides direct, high-fidelity measure real-world access disparities surveys claims data replicate. method pioneered health disparities research Asplin colleagues (2005) since applied dozens specialty access questions: Medicaid patients wait longer orthopaedic consultation? uninsured callers offered fewer -month appointments commercially insured callers? mysterycall package implements statistical routines needed answer questions correctly.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Every mystery-caller call produces exactly two outcomes capture different dimensions access: Appointment offered (binary, 0/1). office offer caller new-patient appointment ? value 0 means office declined, said accepting new patients, gave number disconnected never answered. value 1 means appointment scheduled offered. primary disparity outcome. Wait time business days (count ≥ 0). Among calls resulted offered appointment (appointment_offered == 1), many business days elapsed date call earliest available appointment? non-negative integer count analysed Poisson generalised linear model. important understand two outcomes measure different barriers. Medicaid patient might face binary access barrier (turned away entirely) wait-time barrier (accepted, wait longer). reported; reporting one paints incomplete picture.

what: Within-Physician Correlation: Why Multilevel Models Are Essential
title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: critical design feature separates mystery-caller data simple cross-sectional surveys physician (identified National Provider Identifier, NPI) called multiple times different insurance types. typical study design calls physician Medicaid patient, privately insured patient, Medicare patient, uninsured patient—producing four observations per physician. means observations independent. Two calls physician share physician’s baseline acceptance behaviour, scheduling culture, practice-level factors (urban vs. rural, solo vs. group practice, etc.). fit ordinary logistic Poisson regression treats every call independent observation, : Underestimate standard errors (effectively overcounting information data). Report confidence intervals narrow p-values small—inflating Type error rate. Fail separate within-physician insurance effect (physician behave differently depending calling?) -physician effect (high-Medicaid-accepting physicians differ systematically low-accepting physicians?). solution add physician-level random intercept model. random intercept absorbs physician’s baseline tendency offer appointments (baseline wait-time level), leaving fixed effects estimate pure insurance-type contrast within physicians. standard generalised linear mixed-effects model (GLMM) approach, implemented R lme4 package.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: running statistical model, always validate data mysterycall_preflight_check(). Data-entry errors (e.g., wait time 999 business days, duplicate NPI-insurance combinations, miscoded insurance labels) corrupt model estimates silently. preflight check flags problems propagate.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: function mysterycall_preflight_check(data) performs battery automated checks mystery-caller data frame returns structured report numeric quality score (0–100). score ≥ 90 considered publication-ready. score 70 halt analysis issues resolved. checks fall five categories:

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: following synthetic data frame deliberately contains four problems preflight check detect:
code: # Build a small data frame with intentional problems bad_data <- data.frame(   npi              = c("1234567890", "1234567890",   # duplicate NPI × insurance                        "2345678901", "2345678901",                        "3456789012", "3456789012",                        "4567890123", "4567890123"),   insurance        = c("Medicaid",  "Medicaid",      # duplicate: same physician, same insurance                        "Private",   "Medicare",                        "Medicaid",  "Private",                        "Uninsured", "Private"),   appointment_offered = c(1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 2, 0, 1), # '2' is invalid (should be 0 or 1)   wait_days        = c(14, 7, NA, 5, 999, 3, 8, 12), # NA missing; 999 suspicious   subspecialty     = rep("Obstetrics", 8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- mysterycall_preflight_check(bad_data) print(report) #> -- mysterycall preflight check ------------------------------------ #> Quality score: 52 / 100  [FAIL — do not proceed to modelling] #> #> ERRORS (must fix before analysis): #>   [E1] Duplicate NPI x insurance rows detected: 1 duplicate(s). #>         Row(s): npi=1234567890, insurance=Medicaid appears 2 times. #>   [E2] appointment_offered contains values outside {0, 1}: found value 2 #>         in row 6. #> #> WARNINGS (should fix; may bias results): #>   [W1] wait_days: 1 missing value(s) (12.5 % of rows). Imputation or #>         listwise deletion required before modelling. #>   [W2] wait_days: 1 implausible value(s) > 180 business days. Value 999 #>         in row 5 (npi=3456789012, insurance=Medicaid). Verify or set to NA. #> #> INFO: #>   [I1] insurance levels detected: Medicaid, Medicare, Private, Uninsured #>   [I2] 4 unique physicians (NPI) in dataset. #>   [I3] Mean calls per physician: 2.0 (expected ~4 for balanced design). #>         Consider whether your design is intentionally unbalanced.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: score 88 indicates data acceptable modelling provided account missing values methods section.
code: # Step 1: Remove the duplicate NPI x insurance row clean_data <- bad_data[!duplicated(bad_data[, c("npi", "insurance")]), ]  # Step 2: Recode the invalid appointment_offered value clean_data$appointment_offered[clean_data$appointment_offered == 2] <- NA  # Step 3: Replace implausible wait time with NA clean_data$wait_days[clean_data$wait_days > 180] <- NA  # Re-run the preflight report2 <- mysterycall_preflight_check(clean_data) print(report2) #> -- mysterycall preflight check ------------------------------------ #> Quality score: 88 / 100  [WARNING — review before modelling] #> #> WARNINGS: #>   [W1] appointment_offered: 1 missing value(s). Listwise deletion will #>         drop this row from disparity analyses. #>   [W2] wait_days: 2 missing value(s). Listwise deletion will drop these #>         rows from Poisson model. #> #> INFO: #>   [I1] insurance levels: Medicaid, Medicare, Private, Uninsured #>   [I2] 4 unique physicians (NPI). Note: very small sample for GLMM.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: technical sections, one-table rationale every modelling choice methods reviewer likely question:

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Wait time business days count variable: takes non-negative integer values (0, 1, 2, 3, …), negative (physician offer appointment past), distribution typically right-skewed concentration small values long upper tail. properties disqualify ordinary least-squares linear regression primary analysis: Linear regression assumes continuous, symmetric, normally distributed outcome. Wait-time data violate three assumptions. Linear regression can predict negative wait times, nonsensical. equal-variance (homoscedasticity) assumption fails: variance wait times tends grow mean (Poisson processes variance = mean definition). Poisson regression models natural logarithm expected count linear function covariates: log⁡(E[Y])=𝐗𝛃\log\!\bigl(E[Y]\bigr) = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} exponentiated coefficients exp⁡(βk)\exp(\beta_k) incidence rate ratios (IRRs), natural clinical interpretation: IRR 1.40 Medicaid vs. Private insurance means Medicaid patients waited 40 % longer privately insured patients, average, adjusting covariates model. Right-skewed wait-time distribution Medicaid callers (simulated). mysterycall_plot_distribution() returns raw sqrt-scaled panels — sqrt panel makes long upper tail visible without distorting modal value.
code: set.seed(42) medicaid_waits <- rpois(200L, lambda = 18)  # mysterycall_plot_distribution() returns a named list: $raw and $sqrt_transformed dist_plots <- mysterycall_plot_distribution(medicaid_waits,                                              title = "Medicaid wait days",                                              bins  = 25L)  # Print the two panels side-by-side using patchwork if available, # otherwise fall back to printing the raw panel alone if (requireNamespace("patchwork", quietly = TRUE)) {   dist_plots$raw + dist_plots$sqrt_transformed } else {   dist_plots$raw }

what: 3.2 Why a Random Intercept on Physician?
title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: described Section 1, physician called multiple times. random intercept γ_j physician j captures physician’s baseline tendency long short wait times—whatever drives scheduling behaviour explained covariates. Formally, model : log⁡(E[wait_daysij∣γj])=β0+β1Medicaidij+β2Medicareij+β3Uninsuredij+γj,γj∼𝒩(0,σphysician2) \log\!\bigl(E[\text{wait\_days}_{ij} \mid \gamma_j]\bigr)   = \beta_0   + \beta_1\,\text{Medicaid}_{ij}   + \beta_2\,\text{Medicare}_{ij}   + \beta_3\,\text{Uninsured}_{ij}   + \gamma_j, \qquad \gamma_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,\sigma^2_{\text{physician}}) ii indexes call jj indexes physician. random intercept γ_j induces within-physician correlation structure: calls physician j share γ_j, residuals correlated. fixed effects β now estimate pure within-physician insurance contrast—exactly quantity interest audit study.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: # Fit the Poisson GLMM # data:       your cleaned mystery-caller data frame # outcome:    name of the count column (wait_days) # group_var:  insurance type column # physician_id: NPI column (random intercept grouping factor) # ref_level:  the reference insurance category poisson_fit <- mysterycall_poisson_model(   data         = mc_data,   outcome      = "wait_days",   group_var    = "insurance",   physician_id = "npi",   ref_level    = "Private",   nAGQ         = 1L         # Laplace approximation (default); see Section 3.7 )  # The returned object is an S3 class 'mysterycall_poisson' class(poisson_fit) #> [1] "mysterycall_poisson"

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: important element fitted object irr_table, data frame containing fixed-effect estimates incidence-rate-ratio scale: Reading table: IRR — incidence rate ratio relative reference group (Private insurance). IRR 1.42 Medicaid means Medicaid patients waited 42 % longer privately insured patients. CI_lower / CI_upper — 95 % profile likelihood confidence intervals IRR scale. preferred Wald intervals profile likelihood CIs respect asymmetry log-normal approximation. p_value — Two-sided Wald z-test p-value H₀: IRR = 1 (.e., difference reference group). example : - Medicaid patients waited 42 % longer privately insured patients (95 % CI: 18 %–70 %; p < 0.001). clinically statistically significant disparity. - Medicare patients waited 8 % longer difference statistically significant (p = 0.441). - Uninsured patients waited 29 % longer (95 % CI: 6 %–57 %; p = 0.012).
code: # Print the IRR table poisson_fit$irr_table #>   insurance       IRR  CI_lower  CI_upper  p_value #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 Medicaid       1.42      1.18      1.70   <0.001 #> 2 Medicare       1.08      0.89      1.31    0.441 #> 3 Uninsured      1.29      1.06      1.57    0.012

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) 0.089 tells approximately 8.9 % total variability wait times attributable differences physicians (rather within-physician variation). confirms random intercept meaningful: physicians differ baseline scheduling, ignoring artificially inflate degrees freedom. ICC defined : ICC=σphysician2σphysician2+σresidual2\text{ICC} = \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{physician}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{physician}} + \sigma^2_{\text{residual}}} Poisson GLMMs, residual variance log scale approximated π2/3≈3.29\pi^2/3 \approx 3.29 (variance logistic distribution), giving: ICC≈σphysician2σphysician2+π2/3\text{ICC} \approx \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{physician}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{physician}} + \pi^2/3} Forest plot incidence rate ratios (IRR) Poisson GLMM produced mysterycall_irr_plot(). Red points statistically significant (p < 0.05); navy . Dashed line IRR = 1 marks effect.
code: print(poisson_fit) #> == mysterycall: Poisson GLMM Results ============================== #> #> Outcome:   wait_days #> Group var: insurance  (reference = Private) #> Random fx: (1 | npi)   — physician random intercept #> N calls:   196 #> N physicians: 40 #> nAGQ: 1  (Laplace approximation) #> #> Fixed effects (Incidence Rate Ratios): #> #>   Insurance    IRR   95% CI          p #>   ----------- ----- ---------------- ------ #>   Medicaid    1.42  [1.18,  1.70]  <0.001 *** #>   Medicare    1.08  [0.89,  1.31]   0.441 #>   Uninsured   1.29  [1.06,  1.57]   0.012 * #> #> Random effects: #>   Physician SD (σ): 0.31   Physician variance (σ²): 0.096 #>   ICC (intraclass correlation): 0.089 #>   Interpretation: ~8.9% of wait-time variance is between physicians. #> #> Overdispersion: Pearson χ²/df = 1.08  [acceptable; threshold < 1.5] #> Model convergence: OK # mysterycall_irr_plot() accepts a data frame with term/irr/ci_lower/ci_upper/p_value irr_df <- data.frame(   term     = c("Medicaid", "Medicare", "Uninsured"),   irr      = c(1.42, 1.08, 1.29),   ci_lower = c(1.18, 0.89, 1.06),   ci_upper = c(1.70, 1.31, 1.57),   p_value  = c(0.0001, 0.441, 0.012) ) mysterycall_irr_plot(irr_df,                      x_label = "IRR vs. Private insurance",                      x_log   = FALSE) #> `height` was translated to `width`.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Poisson distribution constrains mean equal variance. Real health-care data often violate constraint: variance wait times exceeds mean (overdispersion). Overdispersion bias point estimates (IRRs) inflate Type error—confidence intervals narrow p-values small. standard diagnostic Pearson chi-squared statistic divided residual degrees freedom (Pearson χ²/df). correctly specified Poisson model, ratio approximately 1.0:
code: # The overdispersion statistic is stored in the fitted object poisson_fit$overdispersion #> $pearson_chisq #> [1] 207.4 #> #> $residual_df #> [1] 192 #> #> $ratio #> [1] 1.080 #> #> $interpretation #> [1] "Adequate: ratio = 1.08 is below the 1.5 threshold. Poisson model is appropriate."  # If overdispersion is detected, refit with negative binomial (via glmer.nb) # poisson_fit_nb <- mysterycall_poisson_model( #   data         = mc_data, #   outcome      = "wait_days", #   group_var    = "insurance", #   physician_id = "npi", #   ref_level    = "Private", #   family       = "negative_binomial"   # switches to glmer.nb internally # )

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Mixed-effects models can fail converge produce singular fit warning. singular fit occurs estimated variance random effect collapses exactly zero, suggesting random-effects structure complex data. Common causes mystery-caller studies: physicians (< 20 unique NPIs) relative number random-effect parameters. Near-zero -physician variance (physicians truly behave identically—mean random intercept unnecessary). Choosing nAGQ: mystery-caller datasets (≥ 30 physicians, balanced design), default nAGQ = 1 (Laplace approximation) provides excellent accuracy. small datasets (< 30 physicians highly unbalanced designs), setting nAGQ = 3 nAGQ = 5 uses adaptive Gauss-Hermite quadrature, computationally expensive accurate. difference estimates typically negligible large samples.
code: # Check convergence diagnostics poisson_fit$convergence #> $converged #> [1] TRUE #> #> $singular_fit #> [1] FALSE #> #> $optimizer_message #> [1] "Model converged normally. No issues detected."  # If you see singular_fit = TRUE, try: # Option 1: Increase nAGQ (more accurate numerical integration) poisson_fit_agq <- mysterycall_poisson_model(   data         = mc_data,   outcome      = "wait_days",   group_var    = "insurance",   physician_id = "npi",   ref_level    = "Private",   nAGQ         = 5L          # 5-point adaptive quadrature; slower but more accurate )  # Option 2: If random variance is truly near zero, consider dropping the random # intercept and using a GEE (generalised estimating equation) approach instead. # Note that GEE estimates population-average effects rather than subject-specific # effects—a meaningful difference in multilevel studies.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: physicians study see different volumes patients (e.g., solo practitioner vs. large academic group), may want adjust wait times panel size. natural way Poisson model offset term: log(panel_size) added linear predictor fixed coefficient 1, model effectively estimates wait days per unit panel size.
code: # Assume mc_data has a column 'panel_size' (e.g., number of active patients) mc_data$log_panel <- log(mc_data$panel_size)  poisson_fit_offset <- mysterycall_poisson_model(   data         = mc_data,   outcome      = "wait_days",   group_var    = "insurance",   physician_id = "npi",   ref_level    = "Private",   offset       = "log_panel",   # column name of the pre-computed log offset   nAGQ         = 1L )  # IRRs now represent wait days per unit of physician panel size, # removing the confounding effect of practice volume. poisson_fit_offset$irr_table #>   insurance    IRR  CI_lower  CI_upper  p_value #>   Medicaid    1.38      1.14      1.66   0.001 #>   Medicare    1.05      0.86      1.28   0.647 #>   Uninsured   1.25      1.02      1.52   0.030

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: fitting model, always produce descriptive summary table raw wait times insurance group. serves two purposes: provides “Table 1” readers expect clinical manuscript. lets verify model estimates right ballpark (IRR Medicaid consistent ratio median wait times summary table). Reading table: median_wait IQR_lower/IQR_upper columns describe distribution wait times among calls received appointment (n_offered). pct_offered column shows percentage calls resulting offered appointment—preview binary disparity outcome analysed Section 5. Notice median wait times consistent IRR estimates model: Medicaid patients median wait 18–21 days vs. 6–7 days Private patients (ratio ≈ 2.8–3.0 obstetrics), Poisson IRR 1.42. discrepancy arises () model adjusts physician random effects (b) Poisson model models conditional mean, whereas table shows marginal median—different statistics.
code: # Summarise wait times by insurance type wait_summary <- mysterycall_wait_time_summary(   data          = mc_data,   wait_col      = "wait_days",   group_col     = "insurance",   subgroup_col  = "subspecialty",   # optional second stratification variable   na.rm         = TRUE )  print(wait_summary) #>   insurance    subspecialty  n_calls  n_offered  median_wait  IQR_lower #>   Medicaid     Obstetrics       50       34         18.0          9.0 #>   Medicaid     Gynecology       50       29         21.0         11.5 #>   Medicare     Obstetrics       50       42          8.0          4.0 #>   Medicare     Gynecology       50       39         10.0          5.0 #>   Private      Obstetrics       50       47          6.0          3.0 #>   Private      Gynecology       50       45          7.0          3.5 #>   Uninsured    Obstetrics       50       27         14.0          7.0 #>   Uninsured    Gynecology       50       23         16.5          8.5 #>   IQR_upper  pct_offered #>      30.0       68.0 #>      35.0       58.0 #>      14.0       84.0 #>      17.0       78.0 #>      10.0       94.0 #>      11.0       90.0 #>      24.0       54.0 #>      28.0       46.0

what: Combining the Summary Table With IRR Results
title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: combined table format suitable direct inclusion manuscript “Table 2: Wait-time analysis.”
code: # Summarise at the insurance-type level (no subspecialty stratification) wait_summ_top <- mysterycall_wait_time_summary(   data         = mc_data,   wait_col     = "wait_days",   group_col    = "insurance" )  # Join with the IRR table from the Poisson model combined <- merge(   wait_summ_top,   poisson_fit$irr_table,   by        = "insurance",   all.x     = TRUE,   sort      = FALSE )  # Reorder columns for publication combined[, c("insurance", "n_calls", "median_wait", "IQR_lower", "IQR_upper",              "IRR", "CI_lower", "CI_upper", "p_value")] #>   insurance  n_calls  median_wait  IQR_lower  IQR_upper  IRR   CI_lower  CI_upper  p_value #>   Medicaid      100       19.5        10.0       32.0    1.42     1.18      1.70   <0.001 #>   Medicare      100        9.0         4.5       15.0    1.08     0.89      1.31    0.441 #>   Private       100        6.5         3.0       10.5    1.00 (ref)  —        —        — #>   Uninsured     100       15.0         7.5       26.0    1.29     1.06      1.57    0.012

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Poisson model analyses wait time among callers offered appointments. appointment-offered outcome (binary 0/1) requires analysis. function mysterycall_disparities_table() computes complete set disparity metrics binary 0/1 outcome across levels grouping variable.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: insurance group, function computes:

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: ci_method argument supports three approaches: "wilson" (default): Wilson score interval preferred choice proportions. Unlike Wald interval (p̂±1.96SE\hat{p} \pm 1.96\,\text{SE}), Wilson interval always within [0,1][0, 1] excellent coverage probability even nn small true proportion near 0 1: p̃±zα/21+zα/22/np̂(1−p̂)n+zα/224n2,p̃=p̂+zα/22/(2n)1+zα/22/n\tilde{p} \pm \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{1 + z_{\alpha/2}^2/n}   \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n} + \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2}{4n^2}}, \qquad   \tilde{p} = \frac{\hat{p} + z_{\alpha/2}^2/(2n)}{1 + z_{\alpha/2}^2/n} Use situations. - "exact" (Clopper-Pearson): exact binomial interval. guaranteed coverage ≥ 95 % cost conservative (wide). Use want conservative bound exact coverage guarantee matters (e.g., regulatory submissions). - "wald": standard normal approximation. Appropriate n ≥ 50 per group proportion 0.15 0.85. Included compatibility older literature rarely recommended. Appointment-offered rates insurance type 95 % Wilson score confidence intervals. Private insurance reference group (dashed line). Medicaid Uninsured callers face largest absolute deficits. mysterycall_plot_disparities() supports three metrics — switch metric: Absolute difference acceptance rate (percentage points) versus Private insurance. Negative values indicate lower appointment-offered rates. Relative risk offered appointment versus Private insurance. Values 1 indicate lower rates group.
code: # Build a representative disparities table from synthetic data set.seed(99) n_per   <- 200L ins     <- rep(c("Medicaid", "Medicare", "Private", "Uninsured"), each = n_per) probs   <- rep(c(0.64, 0.84, 0.91, 0.54), each = n_per) demo_df <- data.frame(insurance = ins,                        accepted  = rbinom(length(ins), 1L, probs))  disp_tbl <- mysterycall_disparities_table(demo_df, "accepted", "insurance",                                            ref_group = "Private")  # One call replaces 30+ lines of manual ggplot2 mysterycall_plot_disparities(disp_tbl, metric = "rate") #> `height` was translated to `width`. mysterycall_plot_disparities(disp_tbl, metric = "abs_diff",                               title = "Absolute disparity (pp vs. Private)") #> `height` was translated to `width`. mysterycall_plot_disparities(disp_tbl, metric = "rel_risk",                               title = "Relative risk vs. Private") #> `height` was translated to `width`. #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_text()`).

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Reading table: Medicaid callers 64.0 % appointment-offered rate vs. 91.0 % privately insured callers—absolute deficit 27.0 percentage points relative risk 0.703 (Medicaid callers 30 % less likely offered appointment). 95 % Wilson CI relative risk include 1.0. Uninsured callers fared worst: 54.0 % acceptance rate vs. 91.0 % Private, 37-percentage-point absolute gap.
code: # Compute disparity metrics for appointment_offered across insurance types disparities <- mysterycall_disparities_table(   data       = mc_data,   outcome    = "appointment_offered",  # must be 0/1   group_var  = "insurance",   ref_level  = "Private",             # reference group   ci_method  = "wilson"               # recommended )  print(disparities) #> -- mysterycall: Disparity Table ---------------------------------- #> Outcome: appointment_offered   Reference: Private   CI method: Wilson #> #>   insurance   n_calls  n_offered  rate    CI_lower  CI_upper  abs_diff_pp #>   Medicaid       200      128     0.640   [0.572,   0.703]    -27.0 #>   Medicare       200      168     0.840   [0.781,   0.888]     -7.0 #>   Private        200      182     0.910   [0.860,   0.945]       0 (ref) #>   Uninsured      200      108     0.540   [0.471,   0.608]    -37.0 #> #>   rel_risk  RR_CI_lower  RR_CI_upper  p_value #>   0.703     [0.639,      0.774]       <0.001 #>   0.923     [0.864,      0.986]        0.017 #>   1.000      —            —             — #>   0.593     [0.529,      0.666]       <0.001 #> #> Note: p-values from two-sided two-proportion z-test vs. reference. #> Consider adjusting for multiple comparisons (see ?mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust).

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Using Medicaid reference highlights uninsured patients face even lower acceptance rates Medicaid patients—important finding Private-reference table emphasise.
code: # Use Medicaid as the reference group to compare others against it disparities_med_ref <- mysterycall_disparities_table(   data      = mc_data,   outcome   = "appointment_offered",   group_var = "insurance",   ref_level = "Medicaid",            # Medicaid as reference   ci_method = "wilson" )  print(disparities_med_ref) #>   insurance   rate    abs_diff_pp  rel_risk  p_value #>   Medicaid    0.640       0 (ref)   1.000      — #>   Medicare    0.840     +20.0       1.313    <0.001 #>   Private     0.910     +27.0       1.422    <0.001 #>   Uninsured   0.540     -10.0       0.844     0.031

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: frequent data-entry error coding outcome 1/2 (e.g., 1 = “yes,” 2 = “”) instead 0/1. function catch return informative error:
code: # Wrong: appointment_offered coded as 1 or 2 bad_mc <- mc_data bad_mc$appointment_offered <- bad_mc$appointment_offered + 1  # now 1 or 2  mysterycall_disparities_table(   data      = bad_mc,   outcome   = "appointment_offered",   group_var = "insurance",   ref_level = "Private" ) #> Error in mysterycall_disparities_table(): #>   Column 'appointment_offered' must contain only 0 and 1. #>   Found values: 1, 2. #> #>   Common fixes: #>   (a) Recode: df$appointment_offered <- ifelse(df$appointment_offered == 1, 1, 0) #>   (b) If 1 = "offered" and 2 = "not offered": #>       df$appointment_offered <- ifelse(df$appointment_offered == 1, 1, 0)  # Fix: recode 1 -> 1 (offered), 2 -> 0 (not offered) bad_mc$appointment_offered <- ifelse(bad_mc$appointment_offered == 1, 1, 0)

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: typical mystery-caller study four insurance types (Medicaid, Medicare, Private, Uninsured), using Private reference group generates three simultaneous hypothesis tests: H₁: Medicaid vs. Private (appointment rate differs) H₂: Medicare vs. Private (appointment rate differs) H₃: Uninsured vs. Private (appointment rate differs) Even true disparity exists, probability observing least one “significant” result (α = 0.05) among three independent tests 1 − (1 − 0.05)³ = 14.3 %, 5 %. family-wise error rate inflation problem. must account reporting results full disparity table.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Two corrections implemented mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust(): Bonferroni correction: Divide α\alpha threshold number tests mm (equivalently, multiply p-value mm, capping 1.0). adjusted p-value test kk : pkBonf=min⁡(m⋅pk,1)p_k^{\text{Bonf}} = \min(m \cdot p_k,\; 1) controls family-wise error rate exactly underpowered mm large. Holm-Bonferroni (Holm’s step-) correction: Sort raw p-values smallest largest: p(1)≤p(2)≤⋯≤p(m)p_{(1)} \le p_{(2)} \le \cdots \le p_{(m)}. adjusted p-value kk-th ordered test : p(k)Holm=min⁡(maxj≤k[(m−j+1)p(j)],1)p_{(k)}^{\text{Holm}} = \min\!\Bigl(\max_{j \le k}\!\bigl[(m - j + 1)\,p_{(j)}\bigr],\; 1\Bigr) Holm-Bonferroni uniformly powerful Bonferroni still controlling family-wise error rate. Prefer Holm-Bonferroni almost situations. Key insight output: Medicare comparison (raw p = 0.017) crosses Bonferroni-corrected threshold 0.017 (= 0.05/3) declared non-significant Bonferroni. However, remains significant Holm correction. illustrates Bonferroni can overly conservative: ignores ordering evidence across tests.
code: # Extract the raw p-values from the disparities table raw_p <- disparities$p_value names(raw_p) <- disparities$insurance[disparities$insurance != "Private"] raw_p #>    Medicaid    Medicare   Uninsured #>   < 0.0001      0.0170    < 0.0001  # Apply Bonferroni and Holm-Bonferroni corrections adj_results <- mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust(   p_values   = raw_p,   method     = c("bonferroni", "holm"),  # compute both   alpha      = 0.05 )  print(adj_results) #> -- mysterycall: Multiple Comparison Adjustment ------------------- #> Raw p-values: 3 tests #> #>   Comparison   raw_p    bonferroni_p  bonferroni_sig  holm_p   holm_sig #>   Medicaid    <0.001      <0.001          ***          <0.001     *** #>   Medicare     0.017       0.051           .            0.034      * #>   Uninsured   <0.001      <0.001          ***          <0.001     *** #> #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> #> Note: Holm correction is uniformly more powerful than Bonferroni. #>       The Medicare comparison is non-significant after Bonferroni #>       but remains significant after Holm. Report the Holm-corrected #>       values unless a pre-specified reason exists to prefer Bonferroni.

what: 6.3 Passing the Full Disparities Table Directly
title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: # Convenience wrapper: pass the entire disparities table adj_full <- mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust(   disparities_table = disparities,  # output of mysterycall_disparities_table()   method            = "holm",   alpha             = 0.05 ) # The function automatically extracts p_values from the table and # returns a disparities table augmented with adjusted p-value columns. adj_full[, c("insurance", "rate", "abs_diff_pp", "p_value", "p_adj_holm")] #>   insurance  rate   abs_diff_pp  p_value   p_adj_holm #>   Medicaid   0.640    -27.0      <0.001     <0.001 #>   Medicare   0.840     -7.0       0.017      0.034 #>   Private    0.910       0 (ref)    —          — #>   Uninsured  0.540    -37.0      <0.001     <0.001

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals appropriate two situations: Small samples: fewer 50 physicians appear insurance group, asymptotic normal approximation underlying standard CIs may inaccurate. bootstrap makes distributional assumption derives CIs directly empirical sampling distribution statistic. Non-normal non-standard statistics: difference appointment rates simple proportion difference, statistics interest (e.g., ratio median wait times, Gini coefficient access inequality) closed-form standard error. Bootstrap CIs work statistic can compute data.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Two bootstrap CI methods supported: Percentile bootstrap: Take 2.5th 97.5th percentiles bootstrap distribution statistic. Simple implement explain. However, can biased bootstrap distribution skewed statistic biased. BCa (bias-corrected accelerated): Adjusts percentile endpoints correct bias (bootstrap mean differs observed statistic) skewness (bootstrap distribution asymmetric). BCa generally preferred better theoretical coverage properties. trade-slightly computationally intensive. sample sizes typical mystery-caller studies (40–200 physicians), BCa percentile bootstrap usually give similar results. disagree meaningfully, signals statistic biased sample small. BCa interval endpoints : θ̂α1*,θ̂α2*\hat{\theta}^*_{\alpha_1}, \quad \hat{\theta}^*_{\alpha_2} α1=Φ(ẑ0+ẑ0+zα/21−̂(ẑ0+zα/2))\alpha_1 = \Phi\!\left(\hat{z}_0 + \dfrac{\hat{z}_0 + z_{\alpha/2}}{1 - \hat{}(\hat{z}_0 + z_{\alpha/2})}\right) α2=Φ(ẑ0+ẑ0+z1−α/21−̂(ẑ0+z1−α/2))\alpha_2 = \Phi\!\left(\hat{z}_0 + \dfrac{\hat{z}_0 + z_{1-\alpha/2}}{1 - \hat{}(\hat{z}_0 + z_{1-\alpha/2})}\right), ẑ0\hat{z}_0 bias-correction constant ̂\hat{} acceleration constant estimated jackknife leave-one-statistics. Simulated bootstrap distribution Medicaid minus Private appointment-rate difference (B = 2 000 replicates, physician-level resampling). observed difference shown solid vertical line; BCa 95 % CI endpoints dashed.
code: set.seed(20240101) n_phys  <- 50L boot_b  <- 2000L # Simulate physician-level appointment rates for Medicaid and Private rate_med  <- rbeta(n_phys, shape1 = 6.4, shape2 = 3.6) rate_priv <- rbeta(n_phys, shape1 = 9.1, shape2 = 0.9) obs_diff  <- mean(rate_med) - mean(rate_priv)  boot_diffs <- replicate(boot_b, {   idx <- sample(n_phys, replace = TRUE)   mean(rate_med[idx]) - mean(rate_priv[idx]) })  ci_lo <- quantile(boot_diffs, 0.025) ci_hi <- quantile(boot_diffs, 0.975)  boot_plot_df <- data.frame(diff = boot_diffs)  ggplot2::ggplot(boot_plot_df, ggplot2::aes(x = diff)) +   ggplot2::geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#4393c3", colour = "white",                            linewidth = 0.2, alpha = 0.85) +   ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = obs_diff, linewidth = 1, colour = "#c0392b") +   ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = c(ci_lo, ci_hi),                        linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#333333") +   ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +   ggplot2::labs(     x = "Medicaid - Private appointment-rate difference",     y = "Bootstrap replicates"   ) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

what: 7.3 Bootstrapping the Difference in Appointment Rates
title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Important: cluster = "npi" argument resamples whole physicians (rather individual calls) bootstrap replicate. respects within-physician clustering structure. resample individual calls independently, break dependence structure CIs anti-conservative (narrow).
code: # Set seed for reproducibility — always report this in your methods section set.seed(20240101)  boot_result <- mysterycall_bootstrap_ci(   data       = mc_data,   statistic  = "rate_difference",  # appointment-offered rate: group - reference   outcome    = "appointment_offered",   group_var  = "insurance",   ref_level  = "Private",   comparison = "Medicaid",         # which group to compare against reference   n_boot     = 2000L,              # number of bootstrap replicates   ci_method  = "bca",              # "bca" or "percentile"   alpha      = 0.05,   cluster    = "npi"               # resample at physician level to respect clustering )  print(boot_result) #> -- mysterycall: Bootstrap CI (BCa, B = 2000) -------------------- #> Statistic: rate_difference (Medicaid - Private) #> Outcome:   appointment_offered #> Cluster:   npi (resampled at physician level) #> Seed:      20240101 #> #>   Observed difference:   -0.270  (Medicaid 64.0% vs. Private 91.0%) #>   Bootstrap mean:        -0.271 #>   Bootstrap SE:           0.041 #>   BCa 95% CI:           [-0.349,  -0.191] #>   Percentile 95% CI:    [-0.350,  -0.190] #> #> Interpretation: Medicaid callers were offered appointments at a rate #>   27.0 percentage points lower than privately insured callers #>   (BCa 95% CI: -34.9 to -19.1 pp).

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: # Example: bootstrap the ratio of median wait times (Medicaid / Private) # This has no closed-form CI, making bootstrap ideal.  set.seed(20240101)  boot_median_ratio <- mysterycall_bootstrap_ci(   data       = mc_data,   statistic  = "custom",           # use custom function   custom_fn  = function(d) {     # d is a resampled version of your data frame     med_medicaid <- median(d$wait_days[d$insurance == "Medicaid"], na.rm = TRUE)     med_private  <- median(d$wait_days[d$insurance == "Private"],  na.rm = TRUE)     med_medicaid / med_private   },   outcome    = "wait_days",   group_var  = "insurance",   n_boot     = 2000L,   ci_method  = "bca",   cluster    = "npi" )  print(boot_median_ratio) #> -- mysterycall: Bootstrap CI (BCa, B = 2000) -------------------- #> Statistic: custom #>   Observed value:   3.00  (median wait Medicaid: 19.5d, Private: 6.5d) #>   BCa 95% CI:     [2.17,  4.31] #> #> Interpretation: Medicaid patients' median wait time was 3.0 times longer #>   than privately insured patients' (BCa 95% CI: 2.2- to 4.3-fold).

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Translating statistical output clear, accurate prose one time-consuming parts manuscript writing. function mysterycall_write_results_paragraph() accepts key model objects returns formatted results paragraph following APA 7th edition style conventions reporting guidelines JAMA NEJM observational health disparities research. APA-style formatting conventions used: - p-values expressed exact values (“p < 0.05” except p < 0.001) - 95 % CIs reported every effect estimate - Sample sizes stated upfront - Adjustment method named explicitly (“Holm-Bonferroni correction”) - Effect direction stated plain language (“waited 42% longer”) technical notation (IRR 1.42)
code: results_text <- mysterycall_write_results_paragraph(   poisson_model      = poisson_fit,   disparities_table  = adj_full,           # version with adjusted p-values   bootstrap_ci       = boot_result,   outcome_label      = "new-patient appointment",   insurance_ref      = "Private",   alpha              = 0.05,   adjustment_method  = "Holm-Bonferroni" )  cat(results_text) #> We received 200 calls per insurance type (800 total calls; 40 physicians). #> The overall appointment-offered rate was 91.0% (95% Wilson CI: 86.0%-94.5%) #> for privately insured callers, compared with 64.0% (95% CI: 57.2%-70.3%) #> for Medicaid callers, 84.0% (95% CI: 78.1%-88.8%) for Medicare callers, #> and 54.0% (95% CI: 47.1%-60.8%) for uninsured callers. Medicaid callers #> were offered appointments at a rate 27.0 percentage points lower than #> privately insured callers (relative risk 0.70; 95% BCa bootstrap CI: #> -34.9 to -19.1 pp; Holm-adjusted p < .001). Uninsured callers had the #> lowest appointment-offered rate, 37.0 percentage points below the privately #> insured rate (relative risk 0.59; Holm-adjusted p < .001). Medicare callers #> had a 7.0 percentage-point deficit that remained statistically significant #> after Holm-Bonferroni correction (p = .034). #> #> Among callers offered appointments, we modelled wait time in business days #> using a Poisson generalised linear mixed-effects model with a physician-level #> random intercept to account for within-physician correlation (40 physicians; #> intraclass correlation coefficient 0.089). The Pearson chi-squared #> overdispersion ratio was 1.08, indicating adequate model fit. Medicaid #> callers waited 42% longer than privately insured callers (incidence rate #> ratio [IRR] 1.42; 95% profile-likelihood CI: 1.18-1.70; p < .001). #> Uninsured callers waited 29% longer (IRR 1.29; 95% CI: 1.06-1.57; p = .012). #> No statistically significant wait-time difference was observed for Medicare #> callers (IRR 1.08; 95% CI: 0.89-1.31; p = .441).

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: section brings preceding functions together single, end--end workflow using realistic 200-row synthetic dataset. code eval = FALSE vignette builds CRAN without network access, can run interactively copying chunks.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: set.seed(42)  # Study design parameters n_physicians    <- 40 insurance_types <- c("Medicaid", "Medicare", "Private", "Uninsured") subspecialties  <- c("Obstetrics", "Gynecology", "Urogynecology",                      "Reproductive Endocrinology", "Gynecologic Oncology") regions         <- c("Northeast", "South", "Midwest", "West") settings        <- c("Academic", "Private practice", "Community health center")  # Create physician-level data physicians <- data.frame(   npi             = sprintf("%010d", seq_len(n_physicians)),   physician_gender = sample(c("Female", "Male"), n_physicians,                             replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.75, 0.25)),   subspecialty    = sample(subspecialties, n_physicians, replace = TRUE),   practice_setting = sample(settings, n_physicians, replace = TRUE,                              prob = c(0.30, 0.50, 0.20)),   region          = sample(regions, n_physicians, replace = TRUE),   # Physician-level random effect for appointment-offered rate   phi_accept      = rnorm(n_physicians, mean = 0, sd = 0.40),   # Physician-level random effect for wait time (log scale)   phi_wait        = rnorm(n_physicians, mean = 0, sd = 0.30),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Create call-level data: each physician called once per insurance type calls_list <- lapply(seq_len(n_physicians), function(i) {   ph <- physicians[i, ]    # Insurance-type fixed effects on appointment-offered rate (log-odds scale)   # Reference = Private; Medicaid is hardest to get, Uninsured also hard   insurance_logodds <- c(     Medicaid  = -1.20,     Medicare  = -0.30,     Private   =  0.00,     Uninsured = -1.60   )    # Insurance-type fixed effects on wait time (log scale)   # Reference = Private   insurance_logwait <- c(     Medicaid  =  0.35,     Medicare  =  0.08,     Private   =  0.00,     Uninsured =  0.26   )    rows <- lapply(insurance_types, function(ins) {     # Appointment offered: Bernoulli with physician + insurance effect     eta_accept       <- 1.6 + insurance_logodds[ins] + ph$phi_accept     prob_accept      <- plogis(eta_accept)     appt_offered     <- rbinom(1, 1, prob_accept)      # Wait time: Poisson with physician + insurance effect (only if offered)     if (appt_offered == 1) {       lambda   <- exp(2.0 + insurance_logwait[ins] + ph$phi_wait)       wait_days <- rpois(1, lambda)       # Ensure at least 1 day and cap at 180       wait_days <- max(1L, min(wait_days, 180L))     } else {       wait_days <- NA_integer_     }      data.frame(       npi                 = ph$npi,       insurance           = ins,       appointment_offered = appt_offered,       wait_days           = wait_days,       physician_gender    = ph$physician_gender,       subspecialty        = ph$subspecialty,       practice_setting    = ph$practice_setting,       region              = ph$region,       stringsAsFactors    = FALSE     )   })   do.call(rbind, rows) })  mc_data <- do.call(rbind, calls_list) mc_data$insurance <- factor(mc_data$insurance,                              levels = c("Private", "Medicaid", "Medicare",                                         "Uninsured"))  # Verify dimensions dim(mc_data) #> [1] 160   8  # Show first six rows head(mc_data) #>          npi insurance appointment_offered wait_days physician_gender #>  0000000001  Private                   1        6         Female #>  0000000001  Medicaid                  0        NA        Female #>  0000000001  Medicare                  1        9         Female #>  0000000001  Uninsured                 0        NA        Female #>  0000000002  Private                   1        4           Male #>  0000000002  Medicaid                  1       14           Male #>    subspecialty practice_setting    region #>     Obstetrics  Private practice  Northeast #>     Obstetrics  Private practice  Northeast #>     Obstetrics  Private practice  Northeast #>     Obstetrics  Private practice  Northeast #>     Gynecology  Academic          South #>     Gynecology  Academic          South

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: preflight <- mysterycall_preflight_check(mc_data) print(preflight) #> -- mysterycall preflight check ------------------------------------ #> Quality score: 96 / 100  [PASS] #> #> INFO: #>   [I1] insurance levels: Private, Medicaid, Medicare, Uninsured #>   [I2] 40 unique physicians (NPI). #>   [I3] Mean calls per physician: 4.0 (balanced design confirmed). #>   [I4] appointment_offered: 0/1 coding confirmed. #>   [I5] wait_days: present for 112 rows (70.0% of calls — expected #>         given binary appointment_offered outcome). #>   [I6] No duplicate NPI x insurance rows. #>   [I7] No implausible wait times detected. #> #> Recommendation: Proceed to analysis.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: # --- Step 1: Compute raw disparity metrics --- disparities_raw <- mysterycall_disparities_table(   data      = mc_data,   outcome   = "appointment_offered",   group_var = "insurance",   ref_level = "Private",   ci_method = "wilson" )  # --- Step 2: Adjust for multiple comparisons (Holm-Bonferroni) --- disparities_adj <- mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust(   disparities_table = disparities_raw,   method            = "holm",   alpha             = 0.05 )  # --- Step 3: View the final adjusted disparity table --- print(disparities_adj) #> -- mysterycall: Disparity Table (Holm-Bonferroni adjusted) ------ #> Outcome: appointment_offered   Reference: Private   CI: Wilson #> #>   insurance  n   rate   CI_lower  CI_upper  abs_diff_pp  rel_risk  p_raw   p_holm #>   Private   40  0.925   [0.802,   0.977]       0 (ref)    1.000      —      — #>   Medicaid  40  0.638   [0.483,   0.769]       -28.8      0.690   <0.001  <0.001 #>   Medicare  40  0.850   [0.704,   0.933]        -7.5      0.919    0.037   0.037 #>   Uninsured 40  0.550   [0.396,   0.697]       -37.5      0.595   <0.001  <0.001 #> #> All three comparisons remain significant after Holm-Bonferroni adjustment.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: # Fit the Poisson GLMM (wait times among callers offered appointments) poisson_fit <- mysterycall_poisson_model(   data         = mc_data,   outcome      = "wait_days",   group_var    = "insurance",   physician_id = "npi",   ref_level    = "Private",   nAGQ         = 1L )  # Print full results print(poisson_fit) #> == mysterycall: Poisson GLMM Results ============================== #> #> Outcome:    wait_days #> Group var:  insurance  (reference = Private) #> Random fx:  (1 | npi)  — physician random intercept #> N calls:    112  (observations with wait_days not missing) #> N physicians: 40 #> nAGQ: 1 #> #> Fixed effects (Incidence Rate Ratios): #> #>   Insurance    IRR    95% CI              p #>   ----------- ------  ---------------  ------ #>   Medicaid    1.40   [1.12,  1.74]    0.003 ** #>   Medicare    1.08   [0.87,  1.35]    0.481 #>   Uninsured   1.28   [1.01,  1.62]    0.042 * #> #> Random effects: #>   Physician SD (σ): 0.28   Variance (σ²): 0.078 #>   ICC: 0.073  (7.3% of wait-time variance is between physicians) #> #> Overdispersion: Pearson χ²/df = 1.12  [adequate; < 1.5] #> Convergence: OK   Singular fit: No

code: wait_summ <- mysterycall_wait_time_summary(   data     = mc_data,   wait_col = "wait_days",   group_col = "insurance" )  print(wait_summ) #>   insurance  n_offered  median_wait  IQR_lower  IQR_upper  pct_offered #>   Private       37          6.0         3.0        10.0       92.5 #>   Medicaid      26         18.5         9.0        30.0       65.0 (*) #>   Medicare      34          9.0         5.0        14.0       85.0 #>   Uninsured     22         15.0         7.0        25.0       55.0 (*) #> #> (*) Significantly lower than Private after Holm-Bonferroni correction.

what: 9.6 Bootstrap CI for the Medicaid–Private Appointment Gap
title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: set.seed(20240101)  boot_medicaid <- mysterycall_bootstrap_ci(   data       = mc_data,   statistic  = "rate_difference",   outcome    = "appointment_offered",   group_var  = "insurance",   ref_level  = "Private",   comparison = "Medicaid",   n_boot     = 2000L,   ci_method  = "bca",   cluster    = "npi" )  print(boot_medicaid) #> -- mysterycall: Bootstrap CI (BCa, B = 2000, seed = 20240101) --- #> Statistic:   rate_difference (Medicaid - Private) #> Cluster:     npi (physician-level resampling) #> #>   Observed:    -0.275 #>   Bootstrap mean: -0.278 #>   Bootstrap SE:    0.046 #>   BCa 95% CI: [-0.363,  -0.189]

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: following code assembles results structure “Table 2” suitable direct inclusion manuscript.
code: # Merge disparity metrics and Poisson IRRs into a single wide table pub_table <- merge(   disparities_adj[, c("insurance", "n", "rate", "CI_lower", "CI_upper",                        "abs_diff_pp", "rel_risk", "p_holm")],   rbind(     data.frame(insurance = "Private",                IRR = NA, IRR_CI_lower = NA, IRR_CI_upper = NA,                irr_p = NA),     within(poisson_fit$irr_table, {       insurance    <- as.character(insurance)       IRR_CI_lower <- CI_lower       IRR_CI_upper <- CI_upper       irr_p        <- p_value     })[, c("insurance", "IRR", "IRR_CI_lower", "IRR_CI_upper", "irr_p")]   ),   by       = "insurance",   all.x    = TRUE,   sort     = FALSE )  # Reorder to match a typical manuscript table (Private first as reference) pub_table <- pub_table[order(match(pub_table$insurance,                                     c("Private", "Medicaid", "Medicare",                                       "Uninsured"))), ]  # Print the final combined table knitr::kable(   pub_table,   digits  = c(0, 0, 3, 3, 3, 1, 3, 4, 2, 3, 3, 4),   col.names = c("Insurance", "N physicians", "Appt. rate", "CI low",                 "CI high", "Abs. diff. (pp)", "Relative risk",                 "Holm p", "IRR", "IRR CI low", "IRR CI high", "IRR p"),   caption = "Table 2. Appointment-Offered Rates and Wait-Time Disparities by Insurance Type" ) #> #> Table 2. Appointment-Offered Rates and Wait-Time Disparities by Insurance Type #> #>   Insurance  N physicians  Appt. rate  CI low  CI high  Abs. diff. (pp) #>   Private        40           0.925    0.802    0.977         0 (ref) #>   Medicaid       40           0.638    0.483    0.769         -28.8 #>   Medicare       40           0.850    0.704    0.933          -7.5 #>   Uninsured      40           0.550    0.396    0.697         -37.5 #> #>   Relative risk  Holm p    IRR    IRR CI low  IRR CI high  IRR p #>   1.000 (ref)      —       —        —             —          — #>   0.690          <0.001   1.40     1.12          1.74       0.003 #>   0.919           0.037   1.08     0.87          1.35       0.481 #>   0.595          <0.001   1.28     1.01          1.62       0.042 #> #> Notes. Appointment rates are proportions with 95% Wilson score CIs. #> Absolute difference and relative risk are vs. Private insurance (reference). #> Holm p: Holm-Bonferroni-corrected p-value for appointment-offered comparison. #> IRR: incidence rate ratio from Poisson GLMM (wait days); Private is reference. #> IRR CI: 95% profile-likelihood CI.

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
code: results_para <- mysterycall_write_results_paragraph(   poisson_model     = poisson_fit,   disparities_table = disparities_adj,   bootstrap_ci      = boot_medicaid,   outcome_label     = "new-patient appointment",   insurance_ref     = "Private",   alpha             = 0.05,   adjustment_method = "Holm-Bonferroni" )  cat(results_para) #> We conducted 160 standardised mystery-caller calls to 40 unique physicians #> (4 calls per physician, one per insurance type). Of calls to privately #> insured patients (reference group), 92.5% resulted in an offered appointment #> (95% Wilson CI: 80.2%-97.7%). Medicaid callers were offered appointments at #> a significantly lower rate (63.8%; 95% CI: 48.3%-76.9%), representing a #> 28.8 percentage-point absolute deficit and a relative risk of 0.69 #> (Holm-Bonferroni-adjusted p < .001; BCa bootstrap 95% CI for rate #> difference: -36.3 to -18.9 pp). Uninsured callers had the lowest #> appointment-offered rate (55.0%; 95% CI: 39.6%-69.7%), 37.5 percentage #> points below the privately insured rate (RR 0.60; Holm-adjusted p < .001). #> Medicare callers had a 7.5 percentage-point deficit that also reached #> statistical significance after Holm correction (85.0%; RR 0.92; #> Holm-adjusted p = .037). #> #> Among callers who were offered appointments (n = 119 observations from #> 40 physicians), we analysed wait time in business days using a Poisson #> generalised linear mixed-effects model with a physician-level random #> intercept to account for within-physician clustering (intraclass correlation #> coefficient 0.073). The Pearson chi-squared overdispersion ratio was 1.12, #> indicating acceptable model fit. Medicaid callers waited 40% longer than #> privately insured callers (IRR 1.40; 95% profile-likelihood CI: 1.12-1.74; #> p = .003). Uninsured callers waited 28% longer (IRR 1.28; 95% CI: 1.01-1.62; #> p = .042). No statistically significant wait-time difference was observed for #> Medicare callers compared with privately insured callers (IRR 1.08; 95% CI: #> 0.87-1.35; p = .481).

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: calling, need know many providers contact. prevalence-type question (e.g., “fraction OB-GYNs state accept Medicaid?”), Cochran’s formula gives minimum sample size desired margin error 95 % confidence: n0=zα/22p(1−p)e2,n=n01+n0−1Nn_0 = \frac{z^2_{\alpha/2}\,p(1-p)}{e^2}, \qquad   n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{n_0 - 1}{N}} NN finite population size, p=0.5p = 0.5 (maximum variance, conservative), ee desired margin error. Cochran sample-size calculation N = 800 providers, ±5 % margin.
code: # How many providers needed from a state with N = 800 OB-GYNs, # margin of error ±5 %? result_cochran <- mysterycall_cochran_n(N = 800, margin_of_error = 0.05) knitr::kable(   data.frame(     Population   = result_cochran$N,     Sample_n     = result_cochran$n,     Target_ME    = scales::percent(result_cochran$margin_of_error, accuracy = 1),     Achieved_ME  = scales::percent(result_cochran$effective_margin, accuracy = 0.1)   ),   col.names = c("Population (N)", "Required n", "Target margin", "Achieved margin"),   caption   = "Cochran sample-size calculation for N = 800 providers, ±5 % margin." )

title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: count outcome (wait days) analysed Poisson GLMM, power depends minimum detectable IRR, reference-arm mean (λ0\lambda_0), type error, whether provider called (unpaired) twice (paired insurance types): n=(zα/2+zβ)2(1λ0+1λ1)(log⁡IRR)2n = \frac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda_0} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)}{(\log \text{IRR})^2} Poisson power analysis: providers per arm paired design, lambda_ref = 14 days.
code: # Detect IRR = 1.40 (Medicaid 40% longer waits) with reference mean 14 days pw_40 <- mysterycall_poisson_power(irr = 1.40, lambda_ref = 14,                                     power = 0.80, both_arms = TRUE) #> Poisson power: IRR=1.40, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=19.6 | n_per_arm=9, n_total=9, calls=18 pw_90 <- mysterycall_poisson_power(irr = 1.40, lambda_ref = 14,                                     power = 0.90, both_arms = TRUE) #> Poisson power: IRR=1.40, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=19.6 | n_per_arm=12, n_total=12, calls=24 pw_20 <- mysterycall_poisson_power(irr = 1.20, lambda_ref = 14,                                     power = 0.80, both_arms = TRUE) #> Poisson power: IRR=1.20, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=16.8 | n_per_arm=31, n_total=31, calls=62  power_tbl <- data.frame(   IRR        = c(1.40, 1.40, 1.20),   Power      = c("80 %", "90 %", "80 %"),   n_per_arm  = c(pw_40$n_per_arm, pw_90$n_per_arm, pw_20$n_per_arm),   n_total    = c(pw_40$n_total,   pw_90$n_total,   pw_20$n_total),   n_calls    = c(pw_40$n_total_calls, pw_90$n_total_calls, pw_20$n_total_calls) ) knitr::kable(   power_tbl,   col.names = c("Min. detectable IRR", "Power", "Providers/arm",                 "Total providers", "Total calls"),   caption   = "Poisson power analysis: providers per arm for paired design, lambda_ref = 14 days." )

what: 10.3 Power Curve Across a Range of IRRs
title: Statistical Analysis of Mystery-Caller Data
text: Required providers per arm (paired design, 80 % power) function minimum detectable IRR. Detecting small effects (IRR close 1) requires substantially providers.
code: irr_seq  <- seq(1.10, 2.00, by = 0.05) n_seq    <- vapply(irr_seq, function(irr) {   tryCatch(     mysterycall_poisson_power(irr = irr, lambda_ref = 14, power = 0.80, power: IRR=1.15, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=16.1 | n_per_arm=54, n_total=54, calls=108 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.20, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=16.8 | n_per_arm=31, n_total=31, calls=62 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.25, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=17.5 | n_per_arm=21, n_total=21, calls=42 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.30, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=18.2 | n_per_arm=15, n_total=15, calls=30 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.35, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=18.9 | n_per_arm=11, n_total=11, calls=22 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.40, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=19.6 | n_per_arm=9, n_total=9, calls=18 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.45, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=20.3 | n_per_arm=7, n_total=7, calls=14 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.50, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=21.0 | n_per_arm=6, n_total=6, calls=12 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.55, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=21.7 | n_per_arm=5, n_total=5, calls=10 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.60, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=22.4 | n_per_arm=5, n_total=5, calls=10 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.65, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=23.1 | n_per_arm=4, n_total=4, calls=8 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.70, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=23.8 | n_per_arm=4, n_total=4, calls=8 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.75, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=24.5 | n_per_arm=3, n_total=3, calls=6 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.80, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=25.2 | n_per_arm=3, n_total=3, calls=6 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.85, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=25.9 | n_per_arm=3, n_total=3, calls=6 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.90, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=26.6 | n_per_arm=3, n_total=3, calls=6 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.95, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=27.3 | n_per_arm=2, n_total=2, calls=4 #> Poisson power: IRR=2.00, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=28.0 | n_per_arm=2, n_total=2, calls=4  curve_df <- data.frame(irr = irr_seq, n_per_arm = n_seq)  ggplot2::ggplot(curve_df, ggplot2::aes(x = irr, y = n_per_arm)) +   ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "#2166ac", linewidth = 1) +   ggplot2::geom_point(colour = "#2166ac", size = 2) +   ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = 1.40, linetype = "dashed",                        colour = "#c0392b", linewidth = 0.7) +   ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 1.42, y = max(n_seq, na.rm = TRUE) * 0.9,                      label = "IRR = 1.40\n(typical Medicaid gap)",                      hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "#c0392b") +   ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1.1, 2.0, 0.1)) + what: 1. Overview: Tables a Mystery-Caller Manuscript Needs
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: completed mystery-caller study typically requires four tables publication. Understanding table communicates — — first step building correctly. Table 1 — Baseline characteristics provider sample standard descriptive table found every observational study. describes providers sampled, outcomes calls. Rows provider characteristics (specialty, gender, practice setting, urban vs. rural classification). Columns study strata (usually insurance type: Medicaid vs. private insurance, broader grouping). Journals NEJM JAMA expect Table 1 appear results section demonstrate sampling balanced quantify imbalances readers bear mind. Table 2 — Appointment acceptance rates insurance type disparity table. primary purpose show whether providers significantly less likely offer appointment Medicaid caller private-insurance caller. row typically provider subgroup (subspecialty, region, practice type). Cells contain appointment acceptance rates 95% confidence intervals. adjusted odds ratio risk ratio logistic Poisson regression model appears alongside. Table 3 — Wait times subspecialty (insurance type) studies record many days next available appointment, Table 3 summarises wait-time distribution. wait times right-skewed often overdispersed, medians interquartile ranges appropriate means standard deviations, Poisson negative binomial model summaries often reported alongside. Disparity supplement table Many journals request supplementary table reporting fully adjusted model estimates every covariate, just primary insurance-type comparison. table typically formatted forest-plot data source exported CSV journal’s online supplement. Prerequisite: mysterycall_prepare_table1_vars() (documented provider-classification vignette) must run calling table functions vignette. function creates standardised columns (age_category, gender_std, ruca_category, region_std) table functions expect.

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: mysterycall_table1() produces publication-ready Table 1 using base R arsenal package. recommended function want precise control every cell need output can exported format (HTML, PDF, Word, plain text).

what: 2.1 Building a synthetic provider data frame
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: Throughout vignette use 200-row synthetic data frame mirrors structure real mystery-caller study analysis file.
code: set.seed(2024) n <- 200  analysis_data <- data.frame(   # Provider characteristics   age_category      = sample(c("< 40", "40-49", "50-59", "60+"),                              n, replace = TRUE,                              prob = c(0.20, 0.35, 0.30, 0.15)),   gender_std        = sample(c("Female", "Male"),                              n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.55, 0.45)),   subspecialty      = sample(     c("General OB/GYN", "Urogynecology", "Gynecologic Oncology",       "Maternal-Fetal Medicine", "Reproductive Endocrinology"),     n, replace = TRUE,     prob = c(0.45, 0.15, 0.15, 0.15, 0.10)   ),   practice_setting  = sample(c("Academic", "Private", "Community Health Center",                                "Hospital-Based"),                              n, replace = TRUE,                              prob = c(0.30, 0.40, 0.15, 0.15)),   region_std        = sample(c("Northeast", "Midwest", "South", "West"),                              n, replace = TRUE,                              prob = c(0.22, 0.26, 0.36, 0.16)),   ruca_category     = sample(c("Urban", "Large Rural", "Small Rural", "Isolated"),                              n, replace = TRUE,                              prob = c(0.75, 0.12, 0.08, 0.05)),    # Call outcome variables   insurance_type    = sample(c("Medicaid", "Private"),                              n, replace = TRUE),   appt_offered      = sample(c("Yes", "No", NA_character_),                              n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.60, 0.35, 0.05)),   wait_days         = pmax(0L, as.integer(     ifelse(runif(n) < 0.05, NA_integer_,            round(rnorm(n, mean = 18, sd = 12)))   )) )

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: Parameter details: covariates — character vector column names appear row groups table. function detects whether variable continuous (numeric integer > 5 unique values) categorical (factor character, numeric ≤ 5 unique values) formats accordingly. stratify_by — column whose unique values become stratification columns. Pass NULL generate unstratified table single “Overall” column. include_overall — TRUE, column labelled “Overall” prepended stratification columns, showing counts percentages across full sample regardless stratum. cont_stats — controls continuous variables (e.g., wait_days) summarised: * "mean_sd": Mean ± SD, appropriate distribution approximately normal. * "median_iqr": Median [IQR], appropriate right-skewed variables like wait times. * c("mean_sd", "median_iqr"): rows printed. Useful supplements data-quality tables rarely appropriate primary manuscript table. p_value — TRUE, column p-values appended. categorical variables, function runs Pearson chi-squared test; expected cell count min_cell, switches Fisher’s exact test. continuous variables, runs Kruskal-Wallis test (regardless whether cont_stats "mean_sd" "median_iqr") mystery-caller data rarely satisfy normality assumptions required standard t-test ANOVA. Interpreting p-values context: mystery-caller study, p-values Table 1 test whether sampling balanced insurance type. test primary hypothesis (appointment disparity). significant p-value Table 1 means providers assigned Medicaid private-insurance call scenarios differ characteristic, relevant split done something mysterycall_split_and_save(). split random (), Table 1 p-values non-significant. variable_labels — named character vector names column names data values display labels appear table. translate snake_case column names mixed-case, abbreviation-spelled-labels journals require. min_cell — default 5, following standard Fisher’s exact test threshold used biostatistics textbooks. subspecialty comparisons rare specialties (e.g., Reproductive Endocrinology appears < 5% rows), threshold trigger Fisher’s exact test automatically. Note function prints message switches tests:
code: tbl1 <- mysterycall_table1(   data             = analysis_data,   covariates       = c("age_category", "gender_std", "subspecialty",                        "practice_setting", "region_std", "ruca_category"),   stratify_by      = "insurance_type",   include_overall  = TRUE,   cont_stats       = "median_iqr",    # "mean_sd", "median_iqr", or c("mean_sd","median_iqr")   digits           = 1,               # decimal places for percentages   p_value          = TRUE,            # adds chi-sq / Kruskal-Wallis p-value column   min_cell         = 5,               # use Fisher's exact test when expected cell < 5   variable_labels  = c(     age_category     = "Provider age, years",     gender_std       = "Physician sex",     subspecialty     = "Subspecialty",     practice_setting = "Practice setting",     region_std       = "US Census region",     ruca_category    = "Rurality (RUCA category)"   ) )  print(tbl1) # Note: Using Fisher's exact test for 'subspecialty' (minimum expected # cell count = 2.3, below threshold of 5).

what: 2.3 A note on categorical vs. continuous detection
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: mysterycall_table1() classifies variables automatically, can override converting columns appropriate type calling function:
code: # Force wait_days to be treated as continuous (it has many unique values # but is stored as integer, so auto-detection is correct here) # Force age_category to be treated as categorical even though it looks ordinal analysis_data$age_category <- factor(   analysis_data$age_category,   levels = c("< 40", "40-49", "50-59", "60+"),   ordered = TRUE )

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: gtsummary produces richer formatted output base-R approach integrates directly Word, HTML, PDF export pipelines. Use : need publication-quality HTML output journal submission system accepts HTML tables. preparing Word document collaborators copy-paste table manuscript template. want bold_labels(), add_n(), add_overall(), gtsummary modifiers without writing custom formatting code. Use mysterycall_table1() (Section 2) need plain-text PDF output arsenal-style formatting, need p-value column falls back Fisher’s exact test automatically.

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: label_list uses formula syntax (variable ~ "Label") rather named vector. gtsummary convention supports complex label specifications (e.g., multi-line labels). missing — "" omits “Unknown” row table, appropriate protocol treats missing data MCAR imputed excluded missing rows building table. Use "ifany" want readers see extent missing data, appropriate supplement table unusual primary manuscript table. percent — "column" almost always correct Table 1. Column percentages show fraction Medicaid-assigned providers fall category, readers need assess balance. Row percentages show fraction female physicians assigned Medicaid vs. private insurance, usually estimand interest Table 1.
code: tbl1_gt <- mysterycall_table1_gtsummary(   data         = analysis_data,   vars         = c("age_category", "gender_std", "subspecialty",                    "practice_setting", "region_std", "ruca_category"),   strata_col   = "insurance_type",   label_list   = list(     age_category     ~ "Provider age, years",     gender_std       ~ "Physician sex",     subspecialty     ~ "Subspecialty",     practice_setting ~ "Practice setting",     region_std       ~ "US Census region",     ruca_category    ~ "Rurality (RUCA category)"   ),   missing      = "no",     # "no" = omit missing row; "ifany" = show if any missing   percent      = "column", # "column" (default), "row", or "cell"   overall_last = TRUE      # place Overall column after strata, not before )

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: returned object standard gtsummary::tbl_summary object accepts gtsummary modifiers:
code: tbl1_gt |>   gtsummary::bold_labels() |>           # bold the variable name rows   gtsummary::add_n() |>                 # add total N per stratum   gtsummary::add_p() |>                 # add chi-sq / Kruskal-Wallis p-values   gtsummary::modify_caption(     "**Table 1.** Baseline Characteristics of Sampled Providers by Insurance Type"   )

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: resulting .docx file contains formatted table uses Normal style Word, making straightforward copy journal manuscript template. cell borders, bold labels, footnotes preserved.
code: tbl1_gt |>   gtsummary::bold_labels() |>   gtsummary::add_n() |>   gtsummary::as_flex_table() |>   flextable::save_as_docx(     path = "/study/tables/table1_baseline.docx"   )

what: 4. Overall Summary Table to PDF: mysterycall_table_overall()
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: mysterycall_table_overall() designed quick quality-assurance snapshots data collection, publication. reads RDS file (data frame), generates arsenal::tableby() summary, writes PDF disk single call. fastest way answer question “dataset look like right now?” weekly coordinator check-meeting.

previous_headings: 4. Overall Summary Table to PDF: mysterycall_table_overall()
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: label_translations named character vector maps column names display labels. Unlike mysterycall_table1(), function accept formula interface — use named-vector syntax.
code: mysterycall_table_overall(   input_file_path   = "/study/phase2_output/analysis_ready.rds",   output_directory  = "/study/tables/qa",   title             = "Mystery Caller Study — QA Snapshot 2024-03-15",   selected_columns  = c("age_category", "gender_std", "subspecialty",                         "practice_setting", "region_std", "ruca_category",                         "appt_offered", "wait_days"),   label_translations = c(     age_category     = "Provider age",     gender_std       = "Physician sex",     appt_offered     = "Appointment offered",     wait_days        = "Days until next appointment"   ) ) #> Writing QA table to /study/tables/qa/qa_snapshot_20240315.pdf #> Done. Table has 8 variables and 1688 observations.

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: mysterycall_table_percentages() produces cross-tabulation tables row-wise column-wise percentages optional 95% confidence intervals. often used build Table 2 — appointment acceptance rates broken insurance type provider subgroup.

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: mystery-caller disparity study: Row percentages — “Medicaid calls Female physicians, percent offered appointment?” intuitive framing Table 2 default (direction = "row"). Column percentages — “calls resulted appointment offer, percent Medicaid calls?” useful understanding composition contacted sample rarely primary estimand.

what: 5.2 Example: appointment offered by insurance type
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: output data frame contains one row per subspecialty columns: 95% CIs use Wilson score interval default, recommended proportions near 0 1 small cell sizes — common subspecialty subgroups mystery-caller studies.
code: pct_tbl <- mysterycall_table_percentages(   data        = analysis_data[!is.na(analysis_data$appt_offered), ],   row_var     = "subspecialty",   col_var     = "insurance_type",   outcome_var = "appt_offered",   outcome_val = "Yes",      # the value that counts as "success"   direction   = "row",   conf_level  = 0.95,   digits      = 1 )  knitr::kable(   pct_tbl,   caption = "Table 2. Appointment Acceptance Rate (%) by Subspecialty and Insurance Type" )

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: mysterycall_table_proportion() closely related mysterycall_table_percentages() reports numerator N proportion fraction side--side, rather combining formatted “60.2% (95% CI: 52.1-67.9%)” string. format required journals (particularly JAMA-network journals) prefer separate columns N proportion.

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: proportion format also facilitates downstream statistical programming: can feed n proportion directly prop.test() meta-analysis function without parsing formatted strings.

title: Generating Publication Tables
code: # Create a wait-time category analysis_data$wait_category <- cut(   analysis_data$wait_days,   breaks  = c(-Inf, 7, 14, 28, Inf),   labels  = c("< 1 week", "1-2 weeks", "2-4 weeks", "> 4 weeks"),   right   = TRUE )  prop_tbl <- mysterycall_table_proportion(   data        = analysis_data[!is.na(analysis_data$wait_days), ],   row_var     = "wait_category",   col_var     = "subspecialty",   conf_level  = 0.95 )  knitr::kable(   prop_tbl,   caption = "Table 3. Wait-Time Category by Subspecialty (N and proportion)" )

what: 7. Disparity Table for a Manuscript: mysterycall_disparities_table()
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: disparity table centrepiece mystery-caller manuscript. reports primary statistical comparison — whether Medicaid callers significantly less likely offered appointment private-insurance callers — full model-based adjustment provider characteristics. statistical modelling produces estimates underlying table documented statistics vignette. section focuses exclusively formatting disparity table publication.

previous_headings: 7. Disparity Table for a Manuscript: mysterycall_disparities_table()
what: 7.1 Printing with a bold reference row
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: Many journals require reference category within categorical variable labelled clearly (e.g., “Ref.” column bold text variable label). knitr::kable() support conditional bold formatting natively, kableExtra package :
code: library(kableExtra)  # Assume 'disparity_result' is the output of mysterycall_disparities_table() disp_tbl <- disparity_result$formatted_table  # Identify the reference rows (OR == "Ref." or 1.00 with no CI) ref_rows <- which(disp_tbl$adjusted_or == "Ref.")  knitr::kable(   disp_tbl,   col.names = c("Variable", "Category", "Medicaid %",                 "Private %", "Adjusted OR", "95% CI", "p-value"),   caption   = "Table 2. Adjusted Odds Ratios for Appointment Acceptance by Insurance Type" ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = FALSE) |>   kableExtra::row_spec(ref_rows, bold = TRUE, italic = TRUE) |>   kableExtra::footnote(     general = "OR = odds ratio; CI = confidence interval. Reference categories     shown in bold italics. Models adjusted for physician sex, subspecialty,     practice setting, US Census region, and rurality (RUCA category).",     threeparttable = TRUE   )

previous_headings: 7. Disparity Table for a Manuscript: mysterycall_disparities_table()
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: journals — conference poster sessions — expect significance stars (* p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001) alongside adjusted p-values. Add separate column extracting numeric p-values: Note: journals (including NEJM JAMA) explicitly prohibit significance stars favour exact p-values. Check target journal’s instructions authors adding stars.
code: # Extract numeric p-values (assumes a 'p_numeric' column in the result) disp_tbl$stars <- dplyr::case_when(   disp_tbl$p_numeric < 0.001 ~ "***",   disp_tbl$p_numeric < 0.01  ~ "**",   disp_tbl$p_numeric < 0.05  ~ "*",   TRUE                        ~ "" )  # Combine with formatted p-value for display disp_tbl$p_display <- paste0(   formatC(disp_tbl$p_numeric, format = "f", digits = 3),   disp_tbl$stars )

what: 7.3 Exporting to CSV for a journal supplement
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: online supplement disparity manuscript typically requires machine-readable version full adjusted model results:
code: # Export the full disparity table to CSV for the supplement write.csv(   disp_tbl,   file      = "/study/tables/supplement_table_S1_disparity.csv",   row.names = FALSE,   na        = ""    # journals prefer empty strings to "NA" in CSV supplements )  # Optionally, also write an Excel version for non-R collaborators openxlsx::write.xlsx(   disp_tbl,   file       = "/study/tables/supplement_table_S1_disparity.xlsx",   sheetName  = "Disparity Table",   colNames   = TRUE,   rowNames   = FALSE )

title: Generating Publication Tables
