what: 9. Writing the Methods and Results Paragraph: mysterycall_write_results_paragraph()
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: mysterycall_write_results_paragraph() generates structured prose paragraph suitable Methods Results sections manuscript. extracts key statistics model result disparity table formats according APA 7th edition number-formatting conventions (two decimal places statistics, three p-values, confidence intervals brackets).

previous_headings: 9. Writing the Methods and Results Paragraph: mysterycall_write_results_paragraph()
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: typical output looks like:
code: paragraph <- mysterycall_write_results_paragraph(   model_result    = poisson_model_result,   # output of mysterycall_fit_model()   disparity_table = disp_tbl,              # output of mysterycall_disparities_table()   outcome_label   = "appointment acceptance",   insurance_var   = "insurance_type",   ref_level       = "Private",   alt_level       = "Medicaid" )  cat(paragraph) Among 1,688 completed calls to 844 unique providers, Medicaid callers were significantly less likely to be offered an appointment than private-insurance callers (59.2% vs. 72.4%; adjusted odds ratio [aOR] 0.61, 95% CI [0.51, 0.73]; p < 0.001). This disparity persisted after adjustment for physician sex, subspecialty, practice setting, US Census region, and rurality (aOR 0.63, 95% CI [0.53, 0.75]; p < 0.001). Among subspecialties, the largest disparity was observed in Urogynecology (aOR 0.44, 95% CI [0.28, 0.70]; p < 0.001) and the smallest in Gynecologic Oncology (aOR 0.81, 95% CI [0.58, 1.13]; p = 0.21).

previous_headings: 9. Writing the Methods and Results Paragraph: mysterycall_write_results_paragraph()
title: Generating Publication Tables
text: function applies following formatting rules automatically: Odds ratios risk ratios — two decimal places (e.g., 0.61, 0.6 0.613). Percentages — one decimal place (e.g., 59.2%). p-values — three decimal places < 0.001 substituted value 0.001 (e.g., p = 0.031, p < 0.001). Confidence intervals — square brackets two decimal places (e.g., [0.51, 0.73]), following APA 7th edition Table 7.21. Large counts — comma-formatted (e.g., 1,688, 1688). Copy paragraph output directly manuscript draft. medical journal style guides compatible APA number formatting statistics; check target journal’s instructions authors unsure.

Appointment Acceptance Rates and Adjusted Odds Ratios",     "by Insurance Type Among Obstetrics & Gynecology Providers"   ) ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = FALSE) |>   kableExtra::footnote(     general = paste(       "OR = odds ratio; CI = confidence interval.",       "Adjusted models include physician sex, subspecialty, practice setting,",       "US Census region, and RUCA rurality category as covariates."     ),     threeparttable = TRUE   )  # Export to CSV for journal supplement write.csv(   disparity_result$formatted_table,   file      = "/study/tables/table2_disparity.csv",   row.names = FALSE,   na        = "" )

code: # Summarise wait times by insurance type using the Poisson model summary # (assumes mysterycall_fit_wait_model() has been run — see stats vignette) wait_summary <- analysis_data |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(wait_days)) |>   dplyr::group_by(insurance_type) |>   dplyr::summarise(     n            = dplyr::n(),     median_days  = median(wait_days),     q1           = quantile(wait_days, 0.25),     q3           = quantile(wait_days, 0.75),     pct_gt28days = round(mean(wait_days > 28) * 100, 1),     .groups      = "drop"   ) |>   dplyr::mutate(     median_iqr = paste0(median_days, " [", q1, "–", q3, "]")   )  knitr::kable(   wait_summary[, c("insurance_type", "n", "median_iqr", "pct_gt28days")],   col.names = c("Insurance Type", "N", "Median days [IQR]", "% > 28 days"),   caption   = paste(     "Table 3. Days to Next Available Appointment by Insurance Type",     "(Completed Calls with Appointment Offered)"   ) )

title: Generating Publication Tables
text: NEJM — Tables must submitted separate files. Use mysterycall_write_table_pdf() table individually, convert EPS TIFF 600 DPI using journal’s figure preparation guidelines (NEJM treats tables figures online submission system). embed significance stars; report exact p-values. Confidence intervals parentheses, brackets. JAMA JAMA Network journals — Accept Word PDF. mysterycall_table1_gtsummary() → gtsummary::as_flex_table() → flextable::save_as_docx() pipeline (Section 3.3) produces JAMA-compliant Word output. JAMA requires Table 1 report “.” (“N” “n”) column header counts. Obstetrics & Gynecology (Green Journal) — Accepts Word. Prefers percentages reported whole numbers proportions > 10% one decimal place proportions < 10%. Set digits = 0 mysterycall_table1() apply custom formatting function small proportions. Green Journal require p-values Table 1 randomised balanced designs.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Mystery-caller studies form audit research trained research assistants — referred field callers lab assistants — pose prospective patients attempt schedule appointments physicians identified national provider database. mysterycall package manages every computational step pipeline, pulling National Provider Identifier (NPI) records writing final analysis-ready data set.

what: 1.1 The full pipeline as a directed acyclic graph
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
code: ┌─────────────────────┐   │   NPI Search        │  mysterycall_search_npi() / NPPES API   │  (taxonomy terms)   │   └──────────┬──────────┘              │  raw provider roster              ▼   ┌─────────────────────┐   │  Phase 1 Cleaning   │  mysterycall_clean_phase1()   │  · dedup names      │   │  · validate phones  │   │  · flag missings    │   │  · generate IDs     │   └──────────┬──────────┘              │  clean roster + audit JSON              ▼   ┌─────────────────────┐   │  NPI Validation     │  mysterycall_validate_npi()   │  (Luhn checksum)    │  mysterycall_check_duplicates()   └──────────┬──────────┘              │  validated roster              ▼   ┌─────────────────────┐   │  Split into         │  mysterycall_split_and_save()   │  Caller Workbooks   │   └──────┬──────────────┘          │  one Excel file per caller          ▼   ┌─────────────────────┐   │  [Field Callers]    │  (human step — callers make calls, fill Excel)   │  make calls,        │   │  record outcomes    │   └──────────┬──────────┘              │  returned Excel workbooks              ▼   ┌─────────────────────┐   │  Phase 2 Cleaning   │  mysterycall_rename_columns()   │  · harmonise cols   │  mysterycall_clean_phase2()   │  · parse outcomes   │   └──────────┬──────────┘              │  standardised outcome data              ▼   ┌─────────────────────┐   │  Quality Check      │  mysterycall_save_quality_table()   │  per-caller stats   │   └──────────┬──────────┘              │  QA snapshot              ▼   ┌─────────────────────┐   │  Coverage Monitor   │  mysterycall_not_contacted_states()   │  · which states     │   │    have zero calls? │   └─────────────────────┘

what: 1.2 Phase 1 vs Phase 2: what each phase does
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Phase 1 happens callers pick phone. job turn raw export NPPES (similar roster) clean, validated list ready handed field callers. Concretely, Phase 1: Strips whitespace normalises letter case provider names. Validates formats phone numbers. Assigns reproducible random study ID every row, including rows NPI missing. Flags data-quality problems (empty names, missing last names, duplicate entries) without silently dropping rows. Optionally duplicates row single provider appears Medicaid private insurance — common design disparity studies. Writes machine-readable audit JSON disk every decision traceable. Phase 2 happens callers return completed workbooks. job harmonise heterogeneous column names inevitably accumulate across five ten individual Excel files normalise free-text outcome values (e.g., “yes”, “YES”, “Y”, “offered” → single canonical code) merged file analysis-ready.

what: 1.3 Two ways to run the pipeline
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Option — One-call orchestration (recommended routine studies): Option B — Step--step (recommended debugging adapting workflow unusual study design): function documented Sections 2–7 can called independently. Results flow one function next ordinary R data frames, can inspect, filter, modify intermediate outputs point.
code: result <- mysterycall_run_workflow(   taxonomy_terms      = c("207V00000X", "207VB0002X"),   name_data           = provider_names_df,   phase1_data         = raw_roster,   lab_assistant_names = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dana", "Eve"),   output_directory    = "/network/study/phase1_output",   phase2_data         = "/network/study/returned_workbooks",   phase2_output_directory = "/network/study/phase2_output",   quality_check_path  = "/network/study/qa/quality_table.csv",   all_states          = state.abb,   verbose             = TRUE )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration

TRUE, every row phase1_data copied: one copy gets insurance = "Medicaid" gets insurance = "Private". processing_flag_is_duplicate column records rows duplicates. id_seed controls random number generator used assign study IDs rows NPI NA. title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Phase 1 adds following processing_flag_* columns returned data frame. columns logical (TRUE/FALSE). flags never used silently drop rows. Every flagged row retained output. flags exist downstream analysts can filter, weight, report data-quality issues without re-running cleaning step.
code: # How many rows were assigned a generated ID? sum(result$processing_flag_generated_id, na.rm = TRUE)  # Which rows have no last name? result[result$processing_flag_no_last_name, c("provider_first_name",                                                "provider_last_name_legal_name",                                                "npi")]

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Every call mysterycall_clean_phase1() writes JSON file output_directory. filename contains cohort hash unique across runs. JSON contains: Key fields: input_rows — number rows phase1_data processing. output_rows — number rows returned data frame. duplicate_rows = TRUE approximately twice input_rows. empty_names_count — number rows name fields blank. rows_retained_pct — always 100 standard runs; value 100 signals rows filtered (happens set drop_empty_names = TRUE, non-default option). quality_metrics — proportion rows non-missing values critical field. Use decide whether roster ready field work.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: National Provider Identifier 10-digit number assigned CMS. last digit Luhn checksum digit — single-digit error-detection code catches single-digit transcription errors transposition errors (e.g., swapping adjacent digits). mysterycall_validate_npi() applies Luhn algorithm every NPI data marks invalid ones.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: roster built web scraping, OCR, manual data entry contain transcription errors. invalid NPI verified NPPES database, meaning may end calling provider exist , worse, provider different specialty intended sample. minutes Luhn validation field work starts prevents problems entirely.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Valid vs invalid — quick illustration: function always returns NPI character string, even input column contains integers. important leading zeros (rare possible) silently dropped numeric storage.
code: validated <- mysterycall_validate_npi(   data       = phase1_result,   npi_column = "npi"    # default; change if your column has a different name )  # The function adds a logical column 'npi_valid' table(validated$npi_valid, useNA = "ifany") #> #>  FALSE   TRUE   <NA> #>     14   1678      2

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: validation, use mysterycall_check_duplicates() identify providers appear multiple times roster. Duplicate entries inflate apparent sample size , entries assigned different callers, result provider called twice — protocol violation IRB applications.
code: dup_report <- mysterycall_check_duplicates(   data       = validated,   id_column  = "npi",   name_cols  = c("provider_first_name", "provider_last_name_legal_name") )  # Rows where npi_count > 1 are duplicate providers dups <- dup_report[dup_report$npi_count > 1, ] cat(nrow(dups), "rows are duplicate NPI entries\n")  # Remove duplicates before splitting clean_roster <- validated[!duplicated(validated$npi) | is.na(validated$npi), ]

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: roster clean validated, must divided among field callers. mysterycall_split_and_save() reproducible, auditable way writes one Excel workbook per caller.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Imagine 500 providers 5 lab assistants. manually copy-paste rows five spreadsheets, process error-prone (rows get skipped duplicated), reproducible (results differ redo ), leaves record assigned . function solves three problems.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: lab_assistant_names — character vector caller names. name becomes separate Excel file: Alice.xlsx, Bob.xlsx, etc. length vector determines many workbooks produced. split_insurance_order — controls order insurance scenarios appear within workbook. provider appears Medicaid Private insurance (duplicate_rows = TRUE used Phase 1), caller see Medicaid row first Private row second within workbook. useful standardising call script order. seed = 1978 — default seed package default since version 1.0 recommended value studies unless protocol specifies otherwise. Changing seed changes providers assigned caller; document non-default seed methods section.
code: workbooks <- mysterycall_split_and_save(   data                = clean_roster,   lab_assistant_names = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dana", "Eve"),   output_directory    = "/study/workbooks",   split_insurance_order = c("Medicaid", "Private"),   seed                = 1978L       # default; controls assignment shuffle )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Providers assigned callers round-robin pattern: means workloads balanced within ±1 provider across callers roster size. assignment deterministic given seed, order rows data, order names lab_assistant_names. Example round-robin workload distribution across five callers 198-provider roster. Differences 1 provider guarantee balanced calling burden.
code: Provider 1 → Alice Provider 2 → Bob Provider 3 → Carol Provider 4 → Dana Provider 5 → Eve Provider 6 → Alice   (wraps around) Provider 7 → Bob ... callers   <- c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dana", "Eve") n_total   <- 198L base_load <- n_total %/% length(callers) extra     <- n_total %% length(callers) loads     <- c(rep(base_load + 1L, extra), rep(base_load, length(callers) - extra))  wb_df <- data.frame(   caller = factor(callers, levels = callers),   n      = loads )  ggplot2::ggplot(wb_df, ggplot2::aes(x = caller, y = n, fill = caller)) +   ggplot2::geom_col(width = 0.6) +   ggplot2::geom_text(ggplot2::aes(label = n), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5,                       fontface = "bold") +   ggplot2::scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", guide = "none") +   ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),                                limits = c(0, NA)) +   ggplot2::labs(x = NULL, y = "Providers assigned") +   ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   ggplot2::theme(panel.grid.major.x = ggplot2::element_blank())

what: 4.4 Verifying the split: no omissions, no duplicates
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: sending workbooks callers, verify every provider appears exactly one workbook:
code: # Read all workbooks back and combine all_workbooks <- lapply(workbooks$file_paths, readxl::read_excel) combined      <- dplyr::bind_rows(all_workbooks)  # Check 1: same number of rows as the input stopifnot(nrow(combined) == nrow(clean_roster))  # Check 2: no NPI appears in more than one workbook npi_counts <- table(combined$npi) if (any(npi_counts > 1)) {   warning("Some NPIs appear in more than one workbook!")   print(npi_counts[npi_counts > 1]) } else {   message("Split verified: every provider appears in exactly one workbook.") }

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: field callers return completed workbooks, returned files must merged standardised. Phase 2. core challenge callers — despite receiving identical templates — often rename columns, insert columns, spell outcome values differently. Phase 2 handles programmatically.

what: 5.1 Why column names are always inconsistent
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Even rigid Excel template, callers rename columns practice: mysterycall_rename_columns() resolves via fuzzy substring matching. provide list target substrings look standardised names replace .

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: target_strings — character vector substrings (case-insensitive) search column names data. function looks substring anywhere column name. new_names — character vector length target_strings. element standardised name assign corresponding substring found. target substring match column data, function emits warning rather error. intentional: callers may legitimately omit optional columns, warning lets decide whether missing column problem without crashing pipeline.
code: # Standardise column names across all returned workbooks renamed <- mysterycall_rename_columns(   data           = raw_returned_workbook,   target_strings = c("appt", "wait", "insurance", "outcome", "caller"),   new_names      = c("appointment_offered", "wait_days",                      "insurance_type", "call_outcome", "caller_name") ) # If no column contains "wait", you get: #> Warning in mysterycall_rename_columns(): #>   Target substring 'wait' did not match any column name. #>   Available columns: npi, provider_last_name_legal_name, appointment_offered, ... #>   Proceeding without renaming for this target.

what: 5.3 mysterycall_clean_phase2(): the full Phase 2 cleaning step
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: data directory path, mysterycall_clean_phase2() reads every .xlsx .xls file directory, applies mysterycall_rename_columns() one individually, row-binds single data frame. recommended approach standard workflow. data already data frame, processed directly. useful already combined workbooks manually want process single file. complete example showing messy column normalisation:
code: phase2_result <- mysterycall_clean_phase2(   data             = "/study/returned_workbooks",  # directory of Excel files   output_directory = "/study/phase2_output",   target_strings   = c("appt", "wait", "insurance", "outcome", "caller"),   new_names        = c("appointment_offered", "wait_days",                        "insurance_type", "call_outcome", "caller_name"),   verbose          = TRUE ) # Simulate two messy returned workbooks alice_wb <- data.frame(   NPI          = c("1234567893", "9876543210"),   `Last Name`  = c("Smith", "Jones"),   `Appt Offered?` = c("Yes", "No"),   `Wait (days)` = c(14L, NA_integer_),   check.names  = FALSE )  bob_wb <- data.frame(   npi            = c("1122334455", "5544332211"),   provider_lname = c("Garcia", "Patel"),   appointment_offered = c("YES", "offered"),   wait_days           = c(7L, 21L) )  # Phase 2 standardises both: # After mysterycall_rename_columns() each workbook will have: #   npi, provider_last_name_legal_name, appointment_offered, wait_days

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: immediately calling period, study coordinators need quick, printable summary showing whether caller track. quality check table provides .

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: output CSV file one row per caller (optionally per insurance stratum) containing:
code: mysterycall_save_quality_table(   data        = phase2_result,   output_path = "/study/qa/quality_table.csv",   strata_col  = "insurance_type"   # optional: stratify rows by payer )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: standard threshold mystery-caller protocols 80% completion. Read table back flag callers threshold: Use table weekly coordinator check-ins identify callers may need retraining whose workbooks may corrupted return.
code: qa <- read.csv("/study/qa/quality_table.csv")  # Flag callers below 80% completion qa$flag <- ifelse(qa$completion_pct < 80, "REVIEW", "OK")  # Print callers needing review knitr::kable(   qa[qa$flag == "REVIEW", ],   caption = "Callers below 80% completion threshold" )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: field work concludes, verify study achieved geographic coverage across intended states. mysterycall_not_contacted_states() identifies states zero completed calls.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: all_states complete expected set states study. sampling frame includes 12 populous states, pass 12 codes. function computes set difference all_states states present data call_outcome non-missing.
code: not_contacted <- mysterycall_not_contacted_states(   data       = phase2_result,   all_states = state.abb           # all 50 states, or a custom list )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
code: # Quick cross-tabulation of states by contact status contacted_states <- unique(phase2_result$state[   !is.na(phase2_result$call_outcome) ])  status_vec <- setNames(   ifelse(state.abb %in% contacted_states, "Contacted", "Not contacted"),   state.abb )  print(table(status_vec)) #> #>  Contacted Not contacted #>         43             7

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Geographic gaps discovered stage can inform second wave calls discussion team whether certain rural states excluded denominator appropriate justification.
code:
# Which specific states need follow-up? cat("States with zero completed calls:
") cat(paste(not_contacted$state, collapse = ", "), "
") #> States with zero completed calls: #> AK, MT, ND, SD, VT, WV, WY  # Subset the roster to those states for a targeted second calling wave followup_roster <- phase2_result[   phase2_result$state %in% not_contacted$state, ]  cat(nrow(followup_roster), "providers in under-contacted states
")

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: standard studies default pipeline appropriate, mysterycall_run_workflow() executes steps correct order returns named list intermediate final artifacts.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: element direct output corresponding pipeline function, can pass result$phase1_result downstream analysis functions just called mysterycall_clean_phase1() directly.
code: names(result) #>  [1] "phase1_result"           "validated_npi" #>  [3] "workbook_paths"          "phase2_result" #>  [5] "quality_table"           "not_contacted_states" #>  [7] "workflow_summary"        "cohort_hash" #>  [9] "audit_trail_path"        "run_timestamp"

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
code: result <- mysterycall_run_workflow(   taxonomy_terms      = c(     "207V00000X",   # Obstetrics & Gynecology     "207VB0002X",   # Urogynecology     "207VX0201X"    # Gynecologic Oncology   ),   name_data           = name_crosswalk_df,   phase1_data         = raw_nppes_export,   lab_assistant_names = c("Alice Nguyen", "Bob Patel",                           "Carol Smith", "Dana Lee", "Eve Gonzalez"),   output_directory    = "/network/obgyn_study/phase1",   phase2_data         = "/network/obgyn_study/returned_workbooks",   phase2_output_directory = "/network/obgyn_study/phase2",   quality_check_path  = "/network/obgyn_study/qa/quality_check.csv",   all_states          = state.abb,   verbose             = TRUE )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: large studies (> 1000 providers) multi-site studies present console, use mysterycall_run_workflow_logged(). functionally identical mysterycall_run_workflow() wraps every pipeline stage structured JSON logging: line .jsonl log file contains: workflow crashes mid-run, log file shows exactly stage failed input state , can restart stage without re-running earlier steps.
code: result <- mysterycall_run_workflow_logged(   # ... same parameters as mysterycall_run_workflow() ...   log_file = "/network/obgyn_study/logs/workflow_run.jsonl" ) {"stage": "phase1_clean", "status": "started",  "timestamp": "2024-03-15T09:22:41Z", "input_rows": 847} {"stage": "phase1_clean", "status": "completed", "timestamp": "2024-03-15T09:22:43Z", "output_rows": 1694, "duration_sec": 2.1} {"stage": "npi_validate", "status": "started",  "timestamp": "2024-03-15T09:22:43Z"} ...

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: mysterycall_run_workflow() returns, first thing inspect result$workflow_summary. data frame one row per pipeline stage: 0.4% row drop split_workbooks phase2_clean (6 rows) typical: reflects rows callers deleted workbooks, possibly realised phone number fax line provider retired. Document exclusions CONSORT flowchart. proceeding analysis, confirm: rows_retained Phase 1 100% (rows silently dropped). NPI validity rate acceptable (> 95% typical NPPES data). Phase 2 completion consistent calling protocol’s expected non-contact rate. cohort_hash result$cohort_hash matches hash stored lab notebook entry wave data collection.
code: knitr::kable(   result$workflow_summary,   caption = "Row counts through the pipeline" ) # Programmatic pre-analysis checks stopifnot(   result$workflow_summary$rows_retained[1] == 100,   mean(result$validated_npi$npi_valid, na.rm = TRUE) > 0.95,   result$cohort_hash == "a3f8c1d2..."   # paste the hash from your lab notebook )

what: 10.1 Always run the preflight check first
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: passing data workflow, run: validates required columns present, checks completely empty data frames, warns potential encoding problems provider names (accented characters, non-ASCII punctuation) cause silent mismatches later pipeline. Addressing preflight warnings costs minutes; discovering six weeks field work costs months.
code: mysterycall_preflight_check(phase1_data = raw_roster)

what: 10.2 Use a network drive for output_directory
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: team members need read access Phase 1 output, split workbooks, Phase 2 output. Placing output_directory shared network drive cloud-synced folder (Box, Dropbox, OneDrive) ensures callers can download workbooks without emailing files coordinators can monitor progress real time. Avoid paths spaces. Use hyphens underscores:
code: # Good output_directory = "/Volumes/ResearchDrive/obgyn_mystery_caller_2024"  # Risky on some OS + R version combinations output_directory = "/Volumes/Research Drive/OBGYN Mystery Caller 2024"

what: 10.3 Store the cohort_hash in your lab notebook
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: cohort_hash single important artifact linking raw data published analysis. Write lab notebook IRB protocol amendment. typical entry looks like: Wave 1 data collection completed 2024-03-22. Phase 1 cohort hash: a3f8c1d2b5e7f091. Audit trail /network/obgyn_study/phase1/audit_a3f8c1d2.json. record lets — journal auditor — reproduce exact dataset used analysis three years study complete.

what: 10.4 For large studies, use the logged variant
title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: studies 1,000 providers, use mysterycall_run_workflow_logged(). Phase 2 cleaning fails corrupted workbook 2 , log file tells exactly workbook caused failure. can fix one file re-run Phase 2 alone rather restarting entire workflow. logged variant also produces timing information stage. large studies, Phase 1 duplicate_rows = TRUE output_format = "parquet" typically fastest combination (5–10× faster CSV > 5,000 rows).
code: # Recommended settings for large studies result <- mysterycall_run_workflow_logged(   phase1_data      = large_roster,   output_format    = "parquet",   # faster than CSV for > 5,000 rows   duplicate_rows   = TRUE,   verbose          = FALSE,       # suppress console output in batch scripts   log_file         = "/logs/workflow.jsonl" )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: Mystery-caller wait times measured business days — weekends US federal holidays excluded. mysterycall_count_business_days() counts business days two dates; mysterycall_business_days() applies calculation every row data frame. Business-day wait times computed mysterycall_business_days().
code: # How many business days between a call on a Monday and an appointment on # Friday of the following week?  (Calendar days = 11; business days = 10) bd <- mysterycall_count_business_days(   start_date = "2024-03-04",   # Monday   end_date   = "2024-03-15"    # Friday two weeks later ) cat("Business days:", bd, "\n") #> Business days: 9 # Apply to a data frame of completed calls calls_df <- data.frame(   npi       = paste0("NPI-", 1:6),   insurance = c("Medicaid", "Private", "Medicaid", "Private", "Medicare", "Uninsured"),   call_date = as.Date(c("2024-03-04", "2024-03-04", "2024-03-11",                          "2024-03-11", "2024-03-18", "2024-03-18")),   appt_date = as.Date(c("2024-03-25", "2024-03-12", "2024-04-01",                          "2024-03-19", "2024-03-27", "2024-04-08")) )  # mysterycall_business_days() column params: call_col, appt_col, result_col calls_with_bd <- mysterycall_business_days(   data       = calls_df,   call_col   = "call_date",   appt_col   = "appt_date",   result_col = "wait_days" ) #> Business days computed for 6/6 row(s); stored in column 'wait_days'.  knitr::kable(   calls_with_bd[, c("npi", "insurance", "call_date", "appt_date", "wait_days")],   col.names = c("NPI", "Insurance", "Call date", "Appt date", "Wait (business days)"),   caption   = "Business-day wait times computed by `mysterycall_business_days()`." )

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: data collection complete, audit caller’s performance proceeding analysis.

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: mysterycall_caller_reliability() computes Cohen’s kappa (categorical outcomes) intraclass correlation coefficient (numeric outcomes) records audited two callers.
code: set.seed(7) # Long-format audit sample: each record appears twice — once per caller n_records <- 30L audit_long <- data.frame(   record_id  = rep(1:n_records, 2),   caller     = rep(c("Alice", "Bob"), each = n_records),   appt_offered = c(     sample(c("Yes", "No"), n_records, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.72, 0.28)),     sample(c("Yes", "No"), n_records, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.68, 0.32))   ) )  rel <- mysterycall_caller_reliability(   data        = audit_long,   caller_col  = "caller",   outcome_col = "appt_offered",   type        = "kappa" )  print(rel) #> -- mysterycall_caller_reliability -- #> Method       : kappa  #> Statistic    : 0.3478  #> 95% CI       : [ -0.0087 , 0.7043 ] #> N pairs      : 30  #> Interpretation: fair (Landis & Koch)

title: End-to-End Mystery-Caller Workflow Orchestration
text: mysterycall_call_productivity() tallies completed, pending, failed calls caller returns summary data frame QC review. Table: Per-caller productivity mysterycall_call_productivity().
code: set.seed(8) n_rows <- 80L prod_df <- data.frame(   caller           = sample(c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dana"),                              n_rows, replace = TRUE),   call_date        = sample(seq(as.Date("2024-02-01"), as.Date("2024-03-31"),                                  by = "day"), n_rows, replace = TRUE),   appointment_offered = sample(c(1L, 1L, 1L, 0L), n_rows, replace = TRUE) )  prod <- mysterycall_call_productivity(   data        = prod_df,   caller_col  = "caller",   date_col    = "call_date",   outcome_col = "appointment_offered" )  knitr::kable(   prod$summary,   caption = "Per-caller productivity from `mysterycall_call_productivity()`." )

title: Compute appointment acceptance rates — mysterycall_acceptance_rate
text: Calculates proportion physicians offered appointment (successfully contacted, depending accepted_col defined), Wilson confidence intervals. groups specified, runs chi-square test Fisher's exact test (cell falls min_cell).

title: Compute appointment acceptance rates — mysterycall_acceptance_rate
code: mysterycall_acceptance_rate(   data,   accepted_col = "contact_office",   group_by = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95,   min_cell = 5L )

title: Compute appointment acceptance rates — mysterycall_acceptance_rate
text: data data frame containing least column named accepted_col. accepted_col Name column recording whether physician offered appointment. Defaults "contact_office", standard Phase 2 column name mysterycall_clean_phase2(). group_by Optional character scalar naming grouping column (e.g. "insurance"). NULL (default), rates computed full dataset test run. conf_level Confidence level Wilson confidence intervals. Default 0.95. min_cell Minimum expected cell count; cells threshold trigger Fisher's exact test instead chi-square. Default 5.

title: Compute appointment acceptance rates — mysterycall_acceptance_rate
text: named list : summary Tibble one row per group (one row overall). group_by non-NULL, first column named grouping variable (character). Remaining columns: n_total (integer), n_missing (integer), n_accepted (integer), n_rejected (integer), rate (numeric, 0-1), ci_lower (numeric, 0-1), ci_upper (numeric, 0-1). test htest object stats::chisq.test() stats::fisher.test(), NULL test run. p_value Numeric p-value extracted test, NA_real_ test run. test_name Character. One "Fisher's exact", "chi-square", "chi-square (small cells)", "none". interpretation Character. One-sentence plain-language summary suitable pasting Results section.

title: Compute appointment acceptance rates — mysterycall_acceptance_rate
text: acceptance column interpreted generously: logical TRUE, non-zero numerics, character strings "yes", "y", "true", "1" (case-insensitive) count accepted. Everything else – including NA – counts accepted.

title: Compute appointment acceptance rates — mysterycall_acceptance_rate
code: df <- data.frame(   contact_office = c("Yes", "No", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "Yes", "No", "No"),   insurance = c("Medicaid", "Medicaid", "Medicaid", "Medicaid",                 "BCBS", "BCBS", "BCBS", "BCBS") ) mysterycall_acceptance_rate(df, group_by = "insurance") #> $summary #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>   insurance n_total n_missing n_accepted n_rejected  rate ci_lower ci_upper #>   <chr>       <int>     <int>      <int>      <int> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 BCBS            4         0          1          3  0.25   0.0456    0.699 #> 2 Medicaid        4         0          3          1  0.75   0.301     0.954 #>  #> $test #>  #> 	Fisher's Exact Test for Count Data #>  #> data:  ct #> p-value = 0.4857 #> alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  0.001607888 4.722931239 #> sample estimates: #> odds ratio  #>   0.156047  #>  #>  #> $p_value #> [1] 0.4857143 #>  #> $test_name #> [1] "Fisher's exact" #>  #> $interpretation #> [1] "BCBS: 1/4 (25.0%, 95% CI 4.6%-69.9%); Medicaid: 3/4 (75.0%, 95% CI 30.1%-95.4%). Fisher's exact: p = 0.486" #>

title: Assess data quality — mysterycall_assess_data_quality
text: Scores data frame completeness validity 0-1 scale, returning overall score detailed list specific issues may affect downstream workflow steps.  Penalties applied missing values required columns (>50% missing = -3 pts, 20-50% = -1 pt), excessive duplicate rows (>10% = -1 pt), non-character NPI name columns.

title: Assess data quality — mysterycall_assess_data_quality
code: mysterycall_assess_data_quality(data, required_columns = c("first", "last"))

title: Assess data quality — mysterycall_assess_data_quality
text: data Data frame assess. required_columns Character vector column names must present sufficiently complete. Defaults c("first", "last").

title: Assess data quality — mysterycall_assess_data_quality
text: named list elements score (numeric 0-1) issues (list issue records severity message fields).

title: Assess data quality — mysterycall_assess_data_quality
text: Quality scored 0-1 scale subtracting penalties maximum 10: Required column > 50\ Required column 20-50\ 10\ Required column character factor type: -0.5 points Final score = max(0, 1 - penalties / 10). Worked examples 1,000-row physician roster required_columns = c("first", "last"): names present, < 10\ 30\ 60\ columns > 50\ 0.70 0.80 thresholds mysterycall_preflight_check() chosen catch severely incomplete data tolerating modest missingness large rosters.

title: Assess data quality — mysterycall_assess_data_quality
code: df <- data.frame(first = c("Jane", NA), last = c("Doe", "Smith")) mysterycall_assess_data_quality(df) #> $score #> [1] 0.9 #>  #> $issues #> $issues[[1]] #> $issues[[1]]$severity #> [1] "warning" #>  #> $issues[[1]]$message #> [1] "Column 'first' has 50.0% missing values" #>  #>  #>  #> $penalties #> [1] 1 #>

what: Map US states to medical society districts and Census regions — mysterycall_assign_region
title: Map US states to medical society districts and Census regions — mysterycall_assign_region
text: Accepts state names (full two-letter abbreviations, case-insensitive) returns corresponding medical society district Census region. Three classification systems supported:

title: Map US states to medical society districts and Census regions — mysterycall_assign_region
code: mysterycall_assign_region(   state,   system = c("acog", "aao_hns", "census"),   na_label = "Unknown" )

title: Map US states to medical society districts and Census regions — mysterycall_assign_region
text: state Character vector US state names two-letter abbreviations. Mixed formats accepted (e.g. c("CO", "Texas", "new york")). system Character scalar specifying classification system. One "acog" (default), "aao_hns", "census". na_label Character scalar returned unrecognized NA inputs. Default "Unknown".

title: Map US states to medical society districts and Census regions — mysterycall_assign_region
text: Character vector length state.

title: Map US states to medical society districts and Census regions — mysterycall_assign_region
text: "acog"  –  ACOG Districts -XII (obstetrics/gynecology) "aao_hns"  –  AAO-HNS Districts 1-8 (otolaryngology) "census"  –  US Census Bureau regions: Northeast, Midwest, South, West

title: Map US states to medical society districts and Census regions — mysterycall_assign_region
code: states <- c("CO", "Texas", "New York", "california", NA)  mysterycall_assign_region(states, system = "acog") #> [1] "District VIII" "District VII"  "District II"   "District IX"   #> [5] "Unknown"       mysterycall_assign_region(states, system = "aao_hns") #> [1] "District 7" "District 6" "District 2" "District 8" "Unknown"    mysterycall_assign_region(states, system = "census") #> [1] "West"      "South"     "Northeast" "West"      "Unknown"

what: Assign call scenarios to generalist providers — mysterycall_assign_scenarios
title: Assign call scenarios to generalist providers — mysterycall_assign_scenarios
text: mystery caller studies, generalist providers called using vignette matches subspecialty present city/state. function groups providers location, detects subspecialty scenarios available locally, assigns scenarios generalists round-robin order calls balanced across scenarios.

title: Assign call scenarios to generalist providers — mysterycall_assign_scenarios
code: mysterycall_assign_scenarios(   data,   location_cols = c("city", "state_code"),   specialty_col = "specialty_primary",   generalist_level,   scenario_map,   id_col = NULL,   scenario_col = "scenario" )

title: Assign call scenarios to generalist providers — mysterycall_assign_scenarios
text: data data frame containing provider records. One row = one provider. location_cols Character vector column names define geographic group (default c("city", "state_code")). Rows grouped columns together. specialty_col Character scalar. Column holding provider's specialty (default "specialty_primary"). generalist_level Character scalar. value specialty_col identifies generalist providers receive scenario assignments. scenario_map Named character vector mapping subspecialty values specialty_col scenario labels. Example: c("Neurotology" = "Neurotology", "Pediatric Otolaryngology" = "Pediatric Otolaryngology"). name value appears specialty_col; value scenario label assigned generalists cities subspecialty present. subspecialty values used names must derive board certification data (e.g. ABOHNS via mysterycall_parse_certification_subspecialty()) must come NPPES taxonomies_desc DAC taxonomy codes. id_col Optional character scalar. Column used sort rows within location group round-robin assignment. NULL (default) existing row order used. scenario_col Character scalar. Name new scenario column create (default "scenario").

title: Assign call scenarios to generalist providers — mysterycall_assign_scenarios
text: data one additional character column named scenario_col.

title: Assign call scenarios to generalist providers — mysterycall_assign_scenarios
text: Non-generalist rows receive specialty scenario. Generalists locations matching subspecialists receive NA.

title: Assign call scenarios to generalist providers — mysterycall_assign_scenarios
code: df <- data.frame(   city          = c("Denver", "Denver", "Denver", "Denver", "Boulder", "Boulder"),   state_code    = "CO",   specialty_primary = c(     "General Otolaryngology", "General Otolaryngology",     "Neurotology", "Pediatric Otolaryngology",     "General Otolaryngology", "Neurotology"   ),   id = 1:6,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) mysterycall_assign_scenarios(   df,   generalist_level = "General Otolaryngology",   scenario_map     = c("Neurotology"               = "Neurotology",                        "Pediatric Otolaryngology"  = "Pediatric Otolaryngology"),   id_col           = "id" ) #> Scenarios assigned to 3/3 generalist rows; stored in column 'scenario'. #>      city state_code        specialty_primary id                 scenario #> 1  Denver         CO   General Otolaryngology  1              Neurotology #> 2  Denver         CO   General Otolaryngology  2 Pediatric Otolaryngology #> 3  Denver         CO              Neurotology  3              Neurotology #> 4  Denver         CO Pediatric Otolaryngology  4 Pediatric Otolaryngology #> 5 Boulder         CO   General Otolaryngology  5              Neurotology #> 6 Boulder         CO              Neurotology  6              Neurotology

what: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for a Summary Statistic — mysterycall_bootstrap_ci
title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for a Summary Statistic — mysterycall_bootstrap_ci
text: level optional grouping column (whole dataset), draws n_boot bootstrap samples replacement, computes summary statistic, returns percentile confidence intervals.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for a Summary Statistic — mysterycall_bootstrap_ci
code: mysterycall_bootstrap_ci(   data,   outcome_col,   group_col = NULL,   n_boot = 2000L,   seed = NULL,   alpha = 0.05,   stat = c("proportion", "mean", "median") )

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for a Summary Statistic — mysterycall_bootstrap_ci
text: data data.frame. outcome_col Character scalar. Name outcome column. stat = "proportion", values must 0/1. group_col Character scalar NULL. Name grouping column. NULL, single "Overall" row returned. n_boot Positive integer (>= 100). Number bootstrap replicates. Default 2000L. seed Integer NULL. NULL, passed set.seed() bootstrapping make results reproducible. alpha Numeric (0, 1). CI uses alpha/2 1 - alpha/2 percentiles. Default 0.05. stat Character. Statistic bootstrap. One "proportion" (mean 0/1 variable), "mean", "median".

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for a Summary Statistic — mysterycall_bootstrap_ci
text: data frame one row per group ("Overall" group_col NULL) columns: group Character. Group label, "Overall" group_col NULL. n Integer. Number non-NA observations used. estimate Numeric. Observed statistic value (proportion, mean, median depending stat). lower_ci Numeric. Lower percentile CI bound alpha/2. upper_ci Numeric. Upper percentile CI bound 1 - alpha/2. n_boot Integer. Number bootstrap replicates used.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for a Summary Statistic — mysterycall_bootstrap_ci
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(   insurance = sample(c("Medicaid", "Private"), 200, replace = TRUE),   accepted  = rbinom(200, 1, 0.6) ) mysterycall_bootstrap_ci(df, "accepted", group_col = "insurance", seed = 1) #>      group   n  estimate  lower_ci  upper_ci n_boot #> 1 Medicaid  89 0.6179775 0.5168539 0.7191011   2000 #> 2  Private 111 0.7387387 0.6576577 0.8198198   2000

what: Business-day utilities for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_business_days
title: Business-day utilities for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_business_days
text: primary outcome mystery caller studies business_days_until_appointment: number Mon-Fri working days (excluding US federal holidays) call date offered appointment date. helpers build bizdays calendar US federal holidays compute column data frame. Computes mysterycall_count_business_days() every row stores result new column. primary way produce business_days_until_appointment outcome column needed mysterycall_poisson_model().

title: Business-day utilities for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_business_days
code: mysterycall_business_days(   data,   call_col = "call_date",   appt_col = "appointment_date",   result_col = "business_days_until_appointment",   calendar = NULL )

title: Business-day utilities for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_business_days
text: data data frame containing date columns call offered appointment. call_col Name column holding call date. Defaults "call_date". appt_col Name column holding appointment date. Defaults "appointment_date". result_col Name new column create. Defaults "business_days_until_appointment", matches outcome name used throughout package. calendar bizdays calendar mysterycall_us_federal_calendar(). Built automatically NULL (default). Pass pre-built calendar calling function many times avoid rebuilding call.

title: Business-day utilities for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_business_days
text: data one additional integer column named result_col.

title: Business-day utilities for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_business_days
code: df <- data.frame(   physician        = c("Dr. Smith", "Dr. Jones"),   call_date        = as.Date(c("2026-02-02", "2026-02-13")),   appointment_date = as.Date(c("2026-02-10", "2026-02-20")) ) if (requireNamespace("bizdays", quietly = TRUE)) {   mysterycall_business_days(df) } #> Business days computed for 2/2 row(s); stored in column 'business_days_until_appointment'. #>   physician  call_date appointment_date business_days_until_appointment #> 1 Dr. Smith 2026-02-02       2026-02-10                               6 #> 2 Dr. Jones 2026-02-13       2026-02-20                               4

title: Compute per-caller productivity metrics — mysterycall_call_productivity
text: Summarises call volume, scheduling rates, time metrics caller mystery caller dataset.

title: Compute per-caller productivity metrics — mysterycall_call_productivity
code: mysterycall_call_productivity(   data,   caller_col,   date_col = NULL,   outcome_col = NULL,   hold_time_col = NULL,   call_time_col = NULL )

title: Compute per-caller productivity metrics — mysterycall_call_productivity
text: data data frame least caller column. caller_col Character scalar: column identifying caller. date_col Character scalar NULL. Column containing call dates. Accepts Date, POSIXct, character (coerced via .Date()). NULL, n_days calls_per_day NA output. outcome_col Character scalar NULL. Binary (0/1) column indicating whether call resulted accepted appointment. hold_time_col Character scalar NULL. Hold time seconds (numeric) "MM:SS" format (character). call_time_col Character scalar NULL. Total call time seconds (numeric) "MM:SS" format (character).

title: Compute per-caller productivity metrics — mysterycall_call_productivity
text: data frame one row per caller, sorted n_calls descending. Columns: caller Character. Caller identifier (values caller_col). n_calls Integer. Total number calls made. n_days Numeric. Count unique call dates. NA date_col NULL. calls_per_day Numeric. n_calls / n_days. NA date_col NULL. n_accepted Numeric. Sum outcome_col. NA outcome_col NULL. acceptance_rate Character. Formatted "XX.X%". NA outcome_col NULL. mean_hold_sec Numeric. Mean hold time seconds. NA hold_time_col NULL. mean_call_sec Numeric. Mean call time seconds. NA call_time_col NULL. attribute total_calls_all (integer) set returned data frame recording grand total across callers.

title: Compute per-caller productivity metrics — mysterycall_call_productivity
code: df <- data.frame(   caller  = c("Alice","Alice","Bob"),   outcome = c(1, 0, 1) ) mysterycall_call_productivity(df, "caller", outcome_col = "outcome") #>   caller n_calls n_days calls_per_day n_accepted acceptance_rate mean_hold_sec #> 1  Alice       2     NA            NA          1           50.0%            NA #> 2    Bob       1     NA            NA          1          100.0%            NA #>   mean_call_sec #> 1            NA #> 2            NA

what: Compute inter-rater reliability between mystery callers — mysterycall_caller_reliability
title: Compute inter-rater reliability between mystery callers — mysterycall_caller_reliability
text: Calculates Cohen's kappa, ICC, percent agreement two callers – caller gold standard – using base R.

title: Compute inter-rater reliability between mystery callers — mysterycall_caller_reliability
code: mysterycall_caller_reliability(   data,   caller_col,   outcome_col,   gold_col = NULL,   pair_col = NULL,   type = c("auto", "kappa", "icc", "percent_agreement"),   alpha = 0.05 )

title: Compute inter-rater reliability between mystery callers — mysterycall_caller_reliability
text: data data frame. caller_col Character scalar: column identifying caller made rating. outcome_col Character scalar: column containing rating/outcome. gold_col Character scalar NULL (default). provided, row outcome_col compared gold_col (two-rater agreement). pair_col Character scalar NULL (default). unique value identifies subject/call multiple callers rated. data reshaped wide (caller x subject matrix) reliability computed across caller pairs. type One "auto", "kappa", "icc", "percent_agreement". "auto" (default): kappa binary outcomes (values {0, 1, NA}), icc numeric continuous outcomes, percent_agreement character/factor outcomes. alpha Significance level confidence intervals. Default 0.05.

title: Compute inter-rater reliability between mystery callers — mysterycall_caller_reliability
text: named list elements: type Character scalar: reliability method used. statistic Numeric: kappa, ICC, percent agreement. lower_ci Lower confidence bound. upper_ci Upper confidence bound. n_pairs Number matched pairs used. interpretation Landis & Koch label kappa ("poor", "fair", "moderate", "good", "excellent"); NA types.

title: Compute inter-rater reliability between mystery callers — mysterycall_caller_reliability
code: df <- data.frame(   caller  = c("A","A","B","B"),   outcome = c(1, 0, 1, 0),   gold    = c(1, 0, 1, 1) ) mysterycall_caller_reliability(df, "caller", "outcome", gold_col = "gold") #> -- mysterycall_caller_reliability -- #> Method       : kappa  #> Statistic    : 0.5  #> 95% CI       : [ -0.3487 , 1.3487 ] #> N pairs      : 4  #> Interpretation: moderate (Landis & Koch)

title: Assess completeness for required data columns — mysterycall_check_data_completeness
text: data data frame assess. required Columns must present non-missing. id_cols Optional identifier columns used compute uniqueness. thresholds Named numeric vector high medium breakpoints 0 1 determining quality tier.

title: Assess completeness for required data columns — mysterycall_check_data_completeness
text: named list three elements: summary tibble::tibble() one row per entry required (plus one uniqueness row id_cols supplied). Columns: column (character), completeness (numeric, proportion 0-1 non-missing values), missing (numeric, 1 - completeness). quality Character scalar: "high", "medium", "low" based mean completeness score relative thresholds. score Numeric scalar. Mean completeness across required columns (uniqueness row id_cols supplied).

title: Assess completeness for required data columns — mysterycall_check_data_completeness
code: df <- tibble::tibble(id = 1:3, value = c(1, NA, 3)) mysterycall_check_data_completeness(df, required = c("id", "value")) #> $summary #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   column completeness missing #>   <chr>         <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 id            1       0     #> 2 value         0.667   0.333 #>  #> $quality #> [1] "medium" #>  #> $score #> [1] 0.8333333 #>

title: Validate no data loss between pipeline steps — mysterycall_check_no_data_loss
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() # Expect no data loss in cleaning before <- nrow(raw_data) clean_data <- mysterycall_clean_phase1(raw_data) mysterycall_check_no_data_loss(before, clean_data, "Phase 1 cleaning")  # Expect deduplication to remove ~10 rows, allow +/-5 before <- nrow(data) dedup_data <- deduplicate(data) mysterycall_check_no_data_loss(before, dedup_data, "Deduplication",                         expected_change = -10, tolerance = 5) }

what: Check Normality and Summarize Data — mysterycall_check_normality
title: Check Normality and Summarize Data — mysterycall_check_normality
text: function checks normality specified variable dataframe using Shapiro-Wilk test provides summary statistics (mean standard deviation normal, median IQR normal).

title: Check Normality and Summarize Data — mysterycall_check_normality
code: mysterycall_check_normality(data, variable)

title: Check Normality and Summarize Data — mysterycall_check_normality
text: data dataframe containing data. variable string specifying column name variable checked summarized.

title: Check Normality and Summarize Data — mysterycall_check_normality
text: named list either: mean, sd Numeric scalars (Shapiro-Wilk p > 0.05, data approximately normal). median, iqr Numeric scalars (Shapiro-Wilk p <= 0.05, data non-normal). Also emits histogram + density plot Q-Q plot via side effects.

title: Check Normality and Summarize Data — mysterycall_check_normality
code: sample_data <- data.frame(   business_days_until_appointment = c(1.5, 2.0, 1.8, 2.2, 1.9, 2.1, 2.3) ) mysterycall_check_normality(sample_data, "business_days_until_appointment") #> Starting normality check and summary calculation for variable: business_days_until_appointment #> Data extracted for variable: business_days_until_appointment #> Shapiro-Wilk normality test completed with p-value: 0.873269208208328 #> The p-value is greater than 0.05, indicating that the data is approximately normal. #> Histogram with Density Plot created. #> Q-Q Plot created. #> Data is approximately normal. Mean: 1.97142857142857, SD: 0.26903708365382 #> $mean[1] 1.971429$sd[1] 0.2690371 #> Summary calculation completed for variable: business_days_until_appointment #> $mean #> [1] 1.971429 #>  #> $sd #> [1] 0.2690371 #>

what: Classify Academic vs. Non-Academic Practice Setting — mysterycall_classify_academic_affiliation
title: Classify Academic vs. Non-Academic Practice Setting — mysterycall_classify_academic_affiliation
text: Combines organization name patterns, optional hospital affiliation data, optional specialty-based adjustments classify academic vs. non-academic practice settings using weighted scoring system.

what: Classify RUCA codes into Urban / Suburban / Rural — mysterycall_classify_ruca
title: Classify RUCA codes into Urban / Suburban / Rural — mysterycall_classify_ruca
text: Rural-Urban Commuting Area (RUCA) classification system assigns numeric codes ZIP codes based commuting patterns population density. function maps numeric codes three categories used healthcare access research:

title: Classify RUCA codes into Urban / Suburban / Rural — mysterycall_classify_ruca
code: mysterycall_classify_ruca(   ruca_code,   urban_max = 3,   suburban_max = 6,   labels = c("Urban", "Suburban", "Rural"),   na_label = "Unknown",   as_factor = FALSE )

title: Classify RUCA codes into Urban / Suburban / Rural — mysterycall_classify_ruca
text: ruca_code Numeric vector RUCA codes (typically integer decimal, e.g. 1, 2, 3.1, 10.6). NA values returned na_label. urban_max Numeric. RUCA codes <= urban_max classified Urban. Default 3. suburban_max Numeric. RUCA codes > urban_max <= suburban_max classified Suburban. Default 6. labels Character vector length 3 giving labels Urban, Suburban, Rural categories order. Default c("Urban", "Suburban", "Rural"). na_label Character scalar returned NA inputs. Default "Unknown". as_factor Logical. TRUE result returned ordered factor levels c(labels, na_label). Default FALSE.

title: Classify RUCA codes into Urban / Suburban / Rural — mysterycall_classify_ruca
text: Character vector length ruca_code values labels ("Urban", "Suburban", "Rural" default) na_label ("Unknown" default) NA inputs.  as_factor = TRUE, returns ordered factor levels c(labels, na_label).

title: Classify RUCA codes into Urban / Suburban / Rural — mysterycall_classify_ruca
text: 2010-vintage RUCA codes standard crosswalk applied 2023 CMS HRSA analyses.  Although newer ACS-based updates exist, 2010 vintage remains widely cited peer-reviewed healthcare access research version bundled package. ZIP--RUCA crosswalk files available USDA ERS (https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/rural-urban-commuting-area-codes). Join data crosswalk first, pass resulting ruca_code column function.

title: Classify RUCA codes into Urban / Suburban / Rural — mysterycall_classify_ruca
code: codes <- c(1, 2, 4, 5, 7, 10, NA) mysterycall_classify_ruca(codes) #> [1] "Urban"    "Urban"    "Suburban" "Suburban" "Rural"    "Rural"    "Unknown"  # [1] "Urban"   "Urban"   "Suburban" "Suburban" "Rural"  "Rural"  "Unknown"  mysterycall_classify_ruca(codes, as_factor = TRUE) #> [1] Urban    Urban    Suburban Suburban Rural    Rural    Unknown  #> Levels: Urban < Suburban < Rural < Unknown

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
text: function reads Phase 1 results data file, performs various cleaning transformation operations, prepares data analysis. ensures required fields present formats column names. Missing NPI numbers handled generating unique random_id.

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
code: mysterycall_clean_phase1(   phase1_data,   output_directory = tempdir(),   verbose = TRUE,   notify = TRUE,   duplicate_rows = TRUE,   id_seed = NULL,   output_format = c("csv", "parquet"),   parent_cohort_hash = NULL )

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
text: phase1_data data frame containing Phase 1 results data. Ensure includes columns like 'for_redcap', 'id', 'names', 'practice_name', 'phone_number', 'state_name', optionally 'npi'. 'npi' missing values NA, random_id generated fallback. 'id' column exists, preserved 'original_id'. output_directory Directory cleaned Phase 1 data written. Defaults tempdir(). audit trail JSON file also saved . verbose Logical. TRUE, progress messages printed cleaning. Defaults TRUE. notify Logical. TRUE, play notification sound completion optional beepr package available. Defaults TRUE. duplicate_rows Logical. TRUE, row phase1_data duplicated retain previous behavior paired insurance entries physician. Set FALSE keep original number rows. processing_flag_is_duplicate column tracks rows duplicates. id_seed Optional integer seed used generating fallback random IDs runs can reproduced without permanently mutating global RNG state. output_format File format use writing cleaned dataset. Supported options "csv" (default) "parquet". parent_cohort_hash Optional character scalar. cohort_hash upstream audit trail (e.g. mysterycall_search_taxonomy() prior cleaning step). supplied, recorded audit JSON parent_cohort_hash enable DAG lineage tracing. Must 64-character lowercase hex string (SHA-256) NULL (default). Ignored silently valid hex digest.

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
text: Invisibly returns cleaned data frame (side effect: files written output_directory).  returned object carries attributes: audit_trail: List containing processing metadata (timestamps, row counts, flags) processing_timestamp: Time processing completed function_version: Version number function (2.0) output_path: Path saved CSV/Parquet file audit_trail_path: Path saved audit trail JSON file returned data frame includes processing flag columns: processing_flag_empty_name: TRUE name empty/whitespace processing_flag_generated_id: TRUE random ID generated (NPI) processing_flag_is_duplicate: TRUE duplicated rows (duplicate_rows=TRUE) processing_flag_no_last_name: TRUE last name extracted

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
text: New Version 2.0: Data Provenance Audit Trail function now includes comprehensive data provenance tracking: Preserves original id npi columns original_id original_npi Adds processing flags (e.g., processing_flag_empty_name, processing_flag_generated_id) Handles edge cases like whitespace-strings safely Saves audit trail JSON file processing metadata Attaches audit trail quality metrics data frame attributes

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
text: Inputs: phase1_data must contain columns: names, practice_name, phone_number, state_name, npi, for_redcap. output_directory must writable; function creates absent. Guarantees: Output rows \(\geq\) input rows (duplicates may flagged never silently dropped unless remove_duplicates = TRUE). Every output row carries processing_flag_* columns documenting reason modification. JSON audit trail written alongside CSV provenance. Fails : Required columns absent phase1_data. output_directory writable created.

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
text: JSON audit trail written <output_directory>/audit_trail_<timestamp>.json always contains required fields (see also tests/fixtures/audit_trail_schema.json): tests/fixtures/audit_trail_schema.json file documents fields required, volatile (excluded snapshot comparisons), conditional. schema stable across patch versions constitutes public API downstream reproducibility tooling.
code: {   "function_name":        "mysterycall_clean_phase1",   "input_rows":           <integer>,   "input_cols":           <integer>,   "input_colnames":       ["names", "practice_name", ...],   "output_rows":          <integer>,   "output_cols":          <integer>,   "empty_names_count":    <integer>,   "no_last_name_count":   <integer>,   "rows_retained_pct":    <number>,   "rows_duplicated":      <boolean>,   "original_npi_preserved": <boolean>,   "quality_metrics": {     "completeness_npi":   <number>,     "completeness_phone": <number>,     "completeness_names": <number>,     "has_processing_flags": <boolean>   },   "start_time":           "<ISO-8601>",   "end_time":             "<ISO-8601>",   "duration_seconds":     <number>,   "r_version":            "<string>",   "platform":             "<string>",   "package_version":      "<string>",   "parameters":           { ... } }

title: Clean Phase 1 Results Data — mysterycall_clean_phase1
text: O(n) number rows. String normalisation via janitor::clean_names() stringr dominates; expect < 1 s 1,000 rows modern hardware.

text: mysterycall_run_workflow() mysterycall_run_workflow_logged()

code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() # Assuming an input path to a CSV file input_path <- "path_to_your_data.csv" required_strings <- c("physician_information", "able_to_contact_office") standard_names <- c("physician_info", "contact_office") cleaned_data <- mysterycall_clean_phase2(input_path, required_strings, standard_names)  # Directly using a data frame df <- data.frame(   doc_info = 1:5,   contact_data = 6:10 ) required_strings <- c("doc_info", "contact_data") standard_names <- c("doctor_info", "patient_contact_info") cleaned_df <- mysterycall_clean_phase2(df, required_strings, standard_names) print(cleaned_df) }

title: Cochran finite-population sample size — mysterycall_cochran_n
text: Calculates number providers sample known directory N providers estimated proportion (e.g. acceptance rate) within margin_of_error 95% confidence. standard Cochran (1977) formula used mystery caller audit studies determine many physicians call specialty directory.

title: Cochran finite-population sample size — mysterycall_cochran_n
code: mysterycall_cochran_n(N, margin_of_error = 0.05)

title: Cochran finite-population sample size — mysterycall_cochran_n
text: N Integer numeric. Total number providers sampling frame (e.g. size specialty directory). margin_of_error Numeric scalar (0, 1). Desired margin error proportions. Default 0.05 (+/-5 percentage points).

title: Cochran finite-population sample size — mysterycall_cochran_n
text: named list elements: n Required sample size (rounded nearest integer). N Input population size. margin_of_error Input margin error. effective_margin Achieved margin error returned n.

title: Cochran finite-population sample size — mysterycall_cochran_n
text: formula : n = N / (1 + N * e^2) e margin error.

title: Cochran finite-population sample size — mysterycall_cochran_n
text: Cochran, W. G. (1977). Sampling Techniques (3rd ed.). Wiley.

title: Cochran finite-population sample size — mysterycall_cochran_n
code: # 369 pediatric otolaryngologists in the directory mysterycall_cochran_n(N = 369)   # ~= 192 #> $n #> [1] 192 #>  #> $N #> [1] 369 #>  #> $margin_of_error #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $effective_margin #> [1] 0.04998306 #>   # 215 neurotologists in the directory mysterycall_cochran_n(N = 215)   # ~= 140 #> $n #> [1] 140 #>  #> $N #> [1] 215 #>  #> $margin_of_error #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $effective_margin #> [1] 0.04991687 #>

title: Count business days between two dates (vectorized) — mysterycall_count_business_days
code: mysterycall_count_business_days(start_date, end_date, calendar = NULL)

text: start_date Date mystery call. Accepts Date, POSIXct, character vector "YYYY-MM-DD" format. Vectorised. end_date Date offered appointment. types accepted. calendar bizdays calendar mysterycall_us_federal_calendar(). NULL (default) calendar covering 2021 - 2036 built automatically.

title: Count business days between two dates (vectorized) — mysterycall_count_business_days
text: Integer vector length start_date.

title: Count business days between two dates (vectorized) — mysterycall_count_business_days
text: Pairs end_date < start_date either date NA return NA. Pairs end_date == start_date return 0.

title: Count business days between two dates (vectorized) — mysterycall_count_business_days
code: # Monday to Friday of the same week = 4 business days mysterycall_count_business_days("2026-02-02", "2026-02-06") #> [1] 4  # Spans Presidents Day 2026 (Mon Feb 16) = 4, not 5 mysterycall_count_business_days("2026-02-13", "2026-02-20") #> [1] 4

code: mysterycall_create_formula(data, response_var, random_effect = NULL)

title: Create a Formula for Poisson Model — mysterycall_create_formula
text: data data frame containing predictor response variables. Factor columns detected automatically included -; non-response columns treated fixed-effect predictors. response_var Character scalar naming count/numeric response (outcome) column data. random_effect Optional character scalar naming grouping column add random intercept (1 | random_effect) term, e.g. "physician". Default NULL (random effects; produces fixed-effects-formula).

title: Create a Formula for Poisson Model — mysterycall_create_formula
text: formula object.  random_effect NULL formula suitable stats::glm(); provided suitable lme4::glmer().

title: Create a Formula for Poisson Model — mysterycall_create_formula
code: df <- data.frame(days = c(5, 10, 15), age = c(30, 40, 50), name = c("A", "B", "C")) mysterycall_create_formula(df, "days", random_effect = "name") #> Creating formula with response variable: days #> Predictor variables identified: age #> Predictor variables after formatting: `age` #> Initial formula string: days ~ `age` #> Formula string with random effect: days ~ `age` + (1 | name ) #> Final formula object created: days ~ age + (1 | name) #> days ~ age + (1 | name) #> <environment: 0x55f4ba94e148> mysterycall_create_formula(df, "days")  # fixed-effects only #> Creating formula with response variable: days #> Predictor variables identified: age, name #> Predictor variables after formatting: `age`, `name` #> Initial formula string: days ~ `age` + `name` #> Final formula object created: days ~ age + name #> days ~ age + name #> <environment: 0x55f4bb7a8438>

what: Calculate drive-time isochrones for a location — mysterycall_create_isochrones
title: Calculate drive-time isochrones for a location — mysterycall_create_isochrones
text: Computes drive-time isolines (isochrones) given point using routing API via hereR package.  Results memoized memory duration R session repeated calls inputs free. Use mysterycall_clear_isochrone_cache() release memory batch.

title: Calculate drive-time isochrones for a location — mysterycall_create_isochrones
code: mysterycall_create_isochrones(   location,   range,   posix_time = as.POSIXct("2023-10-20 08:00:00", format = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"),   api_key = Sys.getenv("HERE_API_KEY") )

title: Calculate drive-time isochrones for a location — mysterycall_create_isochrones
text: location sf point object representing origin location. range Numeric vector drive-time thresholds seconds (e.g. c(1800, 3600) 30- 60-minute isochrones). posix_time POSIXct scalar giving departure time used routing engine.  Defaults "2023-10-20 08:00:00" (weekday morning). api_key API key.  Defaults HERE_API_KEY environment variable.  Obtain free key https://developer..com/.

title: Calculate drive-time isochrones for a location — mysterycall_create_isochrones
text: named list. success: one element per value range, named drive-time threshold seconds (e.g., "1800"), containing sf POLYGON object isochrone geometry. API failure: list single error element (character scalar) containing error message. Detect failures (!.null(result$error)) { ... }.

title: Calculate drive-time isochrones for a location — mysterycall_create_isochrones
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() location <- sf::st_sfc(sf::st_point(c(-73.987, 40.757)), crs = 4326) isolines <- mysterycall_create_isochrones(   location = location,   range    = c(1800, 3600) ) mysterycall_clear_isochrone_cache() }

what: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: level grouping column (e.g., insurance type), compute acceptance rates, confidence intervals, disparity metrics relative reference group.

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
code: mysterycall_disparities_table(   data,   outcome_col,   group_col,   ref_group = NULL,   ci_method = c("wilson", "exact", "wald"),   alpha = 0.05 )

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: data data.frame containing study data. outcome_col Character scalar. Name binary outcome column (values must 0/1; NAs dropped calculations). group_col Character scalar. Name grouping column. ref_group Character scalar NULL. Reference group label. NULL, uses first factor level group_col factor, otherwise alphabetically first level. ci_method One "wilson" (default), "exact", "wald". alpha Numeric (0, 1). Significance level. Default 0.05 yields 95% CIs.

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: data.frame class c("mysterycall_disparities_table", "data.frame") sorted reference group first, remaining groups descending acceptance rate. Attributes ref_group, ci_method, alpha attached. Columns: group Group label. n Total observations group. n_accepted Sum outcome_col (excluding NAs). rate Acceptance rate (n_accepted / n). lower_ci Lower bound rate CI. upper_ci Upper bound rate CI. abs_diff Difference percentage points ref group (0 reference). rel_risk Rate relative ref group rate (1 reference). rr_lower Lower bound RR CI via log method (NA reference). rr_upper Upper bound RR CI via log method (NA reference). p_value Two-proportion z-test p-value vs. reference (NA reference). p_value_fmt Formatted p-value string.

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: Relative-risk CIs use log method 0.5 continuity correction n_accepted 0: \(SE(\log RR) = \sqrt{1/n_+ - 1/n + 1/n_{ref,+} - 1/n_{ref}}\).

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
code: set.seed(1) df <- data.frame(   insurance = sample(c("Medicaid", "Private", "Medicare"), 120, replace = TRUE),   accepted  = rbinom(120, 1, 0.5) ) mysterycall_disparities_table(df, "accepted", "insurance") #> Disparity table -- 3 groups | ref: 'Medicaid' | wilson 95% CI #> Group                       n  n_acc     Rate  95% CI            Abs.Diff  RR (95% CI)             p-value #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #> Medicaid                   38     18    47.4%  32.5%-62.7%          (ref)  1.00 (ref)              (ref) #> Private                    44     21    47.7%  33.8%-62.1%        +0.4 pp  1.01 (0.64-1.59)        0.974 #> Medicare                   38     18    47.4%  32.5%-62.7%        +0.0 pp  1.00 (0.62-1.61)        1.000

what: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
text: Calls mysterycall_poisson_power() across range incidence rate ratios returns ggplot2 line chart showing required sample size per arm changes effect size. Useful Figure 1 mystery-caller manuscript.

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
code: mysterycall_equation_figure(   lambda0 = 14,   irr_seq = seq(1.1, 2, by = 0.05),   alpha = 0.05,   power = 0.8,   both_arms = TRUE )

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
text: lambda0 Numeric. Baseline expected count reference arm (e.g. mean business days reference insurance). Default 14. irr_seq Numeric vector IRR values evaluate. Default seq(1.10, 2.0, = 0.05). alpha Numeric. Two-sided type error rate. Default 0.05. power Numeric. Desired statistical power. Default 0.80. both_arms Logical. TRUE (default), arm receives n patients; total study size 2n.

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
text: ggplot object. x-axis IRR, y-axis required sample size per arm, vertical dashed line marks IRR = 1 (effect).

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
code: mysterycall_equation_figure(lambda0 = 14, irr_seq = seq(1.1, 1.8, 0.1)) #> Poisson power: IRR=1.10, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=15.4 | n_per_arm=118, n_total=118, calls=236 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.20, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=16.8 | n_per_arm=31, n_total=31, calls=62 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.30, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=18.2 | n_per_arm=15, n_total=15, calls=30 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.40, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=19.6 | n_per_arm=9, n_total=9, calls=18 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.50, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=21.0 | n_per_arm=6, n_total=6, calls=12 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.60, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=22.4 | n_per_arm=5, n_total=5, calls=10 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.70, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=23.8 | n_per_arm=4, n_total=4, calls=8 #> Poisson power: IRR=1.80, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=25.2 | n_per_arm=3, n_total=3, calls=6

title: Estimate workflow resources — mysterycall_estimate_resources
text: Projects approximate runtime memory requirements mystery caller workflow based number input rows.

title: Estimate workflow resources — mysterycall_estimate_resources
code: mysterycall_estimate_resources(n_rows)

title: Estimate workflow resources — mysterycall_estimate_resources
text: n_rows Integer number input rows.

title: Estimate workflow resources — mysterycall_estimate_resources
text: named list elements: runtime_seconds numeric. Total estimated wall time seconds. runtime_hours numeric. Total estimated wall time hours. runtime_str character. Human-readable runtime string (e.g., "45 minutes" "2h 15m"). memory_mb numeric. Estimated peak memory megabytes. memory_gb numeric. Estimated peak memory gigabytes. memory_str character. Human-readable memory string (e.g., "1.5 GB" "700 MB").

title: Estimate workflow resources — mysterycall_estimate_resources
text: Runtime estimated n_rows x 4.5 seconds: 1.5 s/row NPI registry lookups, 0.5 s/row geocoding (assumes warm Google Maps cache), 2.5 s/row isochrone generation. Memory (n_rows x 2) + 500 MB. Reference values full workflow: constants reflect typical US residential broadband warm API caches. Slow networks cold caches can triple runtime estimate. Benchmark 50-100 rows committing large run. subset pipeline used (e.g., NPI lookup ), relevant per-row constant 1.5 s (NPI), 0.5 s (geocoding), 2.5 s (isochrones), combined 4.5 s.

title: Estimate workflow resources — mysterycall_estimate_resources
code: mysterycall_estimate_resources(500) #> $runtime_seconds #> [1] 2250 #>  #> $runtime_hours #> [1] 0.625 #>  #> $runtime_str #> [1] "38 minutes" #>  #> $memory_mb #> [1] 1500 #>  #> $memory_gb #> [1] 1.464844 #>  #> $memory_str #> [1] "1.5 GB" #>

text: x Character vector raw physician name strings. format Character scalar controlling output format: "dr_last" "Dr. Smith" (default). "last" "Smith". "full_clean" Full name credentials titles removed.

text: Character vector length x. NA blank/NA inputs.
